CMV: Being invested in a celebrity’s love life is creepy and should be societally condemned I think tabloid culture has been problematically normalized where it’s just accepted that people should stalk celebrity’s lives by having a camera pointed at them wherever they are. So paparazzi members will just disregard celebrity’s privacy to get that one shot of them with another to the allure of the audience.
But I think even past stalking, just generally caring about who a celebrity is dating is parasocial and weird. I think when they are acting a character it is fair to be invested in the story and who they are with, but when their art becomes blurred with their personal than I think it is just weird. I also feel like this is all pretty self evident and am confused by how people justify keeping up with tabloids and talking about celebrities being in relationships like their friends being in relationships.

Would you equally condemn all gossip? For example, if neighbours are discussing the love life of someone in the neighbourhood (they’re not friends), should this be societally condemned as parasocial and weird?
I think we are hardwired to be curious and care about individuals we know and it is usually a benefit to creating large social structures.
It’s just that the age of technology has widely expanded the physical range of how we can “know” somebody

!delta, gossip is a good point. I do think still that celebrity culture is especially problematic to the extent that gossiping about your friend/neighbour (which can sometimes be problematic) is exponentially worse when it is millions upon millions of people soing it.

CMV: Being invested in a celebrity’s love life is creepy and should be societally condemned I think tabloid culture has been problematically normalized where it’s just accepted that people should stalk celebrity’s lives by having a camera pointed at them wherever they are. I also feel like this is all pretty self evident and am confused by how people justify keeping up with tabloids and talking about celebrities being in relationships like their friends being in relationships.

Generally I'm not in favor of condemning someone for doing something that doesn't hurt anyone, just because I think it's weird. That's not my place to tell others what to do with their lives.
You could make an argument that aggressive paparazzi are hurting someone. But so long as the paparazzi are ethical and the celebrity is cooperating of their own free will, I don't see what the big deal is and maybe you should mind your own business.

Celebrity culture has absolutely had negative impacts. For celebrities, it has led to mental health issues with every single aspect of their lives being surveilled. And for viewers it had led to problematic parasocial complexes that also have mental health consequences. This is not a healthy culture.
Also just because someone can do something, doesn’t mean it’s uncomfortable and weird and shouldnt be criticized.

Right, which is why I specified unethical and aggressive paparazzi as being a negative thing. But if Zendaya and Tom Holland want to make an announcement during an interview that they got married, who cares if someone goes "huh, good for them!"
What you're really saying is that having unhealthy fixations is unhealthy. Which is more of a 'well, duh' thing than anything else. Most people are completely fine occassionally dabbling into celebrity gossip and not forming parasocial relationships that negatively affect their mental health.
Also just because someone can do something, doesn’t mean it’s uncomfortable and weird and shouldnt be criticized.
It can be weird, it can be uncomfortable, you lose me at it should be criticized. Criticized how? By who? Like, bronies make me feel weird and uncomfortable. But if they're not hurting anyone, what business is it of anyone else? The world is full of weird uncomfortable people, you gotta live and let live.

Speaking about “dabbling” in celebrities day by day lives presupposes a problematically normalized parasocial relationship. They have no idea who you are and you have no idea who they are. Reaching a point where their lives have become common knowledge is in itself problematic. They are not our friends and we are not theirs.

Saying something is problematic suggests that it's socially questionable. Since society is largely okay with being curious about the public lives of celebrities, what would you say the problem is exactly? How does the 'problem' manifest as being a negative force in peoples' lives?
Because from where I'm standing, it's just curiosity. And if there are no negative effects of that curiosity, then there are no negative effects of that curiosity.

Celebrity culture has absolutely had negative impacts. For celebrities, it has led to mental health issues with every single aspect of their lives being surveilled. And for viewers it had led to problematic parasocial complexes that also have mental health consequences. This is not a healthy culture.

It can cause issues but for the vast majority of people it doesn't. This really feels more like you not being into it and thinking it's weird vs some greater concern for fellow man. And if that's the case, you probably just need to get over it and mind your own business.

CMV: If you "don't support" homosexuality because of your religion or otherwise, you're still homophobic. This submission was inspired by a post I saw on TikTok (of course), of a girl saying not supporting homosexuality because of your religious beliefs doesn't make someone homophobic. All the top comments were agreeing and quite frankly, I can't fathom why.
I'm operating under the assumption that "not supporting" something means that you disapprove of or oppose it. This often stems from disagreement, a belief it's wrong, or personal reasons like fear of it.
If your religion goes against same-sex relationships, I'm not here to tell you you're a horrible person. But you're still homophobic. Don't deny it just to make yourself feel better.
edit— Homophobia is a dislike of or prejudice against homosexuality. Stop trying to pick apart the word and convince me homophobia means ”fear of the gays”
edit2— I'm turning off notifs now. You can argue amongst yourselves if you wish.

I’ll take a crack at it from a Christianity perspective. (Formerly religious).
Christians are not supposed to practice homosexuality in the same way that they are not supposed to have premarital sex or get divorced. This governs their own personal behavior. It has no bearing on how non-Christians have to act. It is completely possible for a Christian to have no issue whatsoever with homosexuality in the same way that they don’t have a problem with other people getting divorced or having sex before marriage.
A good analogy is how some religions prohibit eating certain “unclean” foods. Does that mean they believe other people who eat those foods are unclean? No. It is a principle they have chosen to live by. Eating the food would make THEM unclean. Others are not governed by the same principles.
To specifically address the “why is homosexuality sinful” question, it’s important to look at the origins of Christian marriage. Under Christianity, there is no imperative to continue the human species. The purest way to live is actually to remain celibate and focus on living a Christ-like life. Men and women are permitted to marry as a concession to their lustful nature to prevent them from taking multiple sexual partners. In other words, marriage is just a less-sinful container for human lust. It isn’t being gay that’s considered wrong, it’s specifically breaking away from the provided mold.
That being said, it is absolutely a recipe for homophobic disaster. Most Christians I grew up with were homophobic in the hateful sense. The above is how I managed to avoid falling into the same bullshit.

!delta
Others are not governed by the same principles.
You bring up a really interesting point with this, it's absolutely possible that there are some who think from a "If I participate in this i'll be punished, but non-religious people aren't bound by the same rules" mindset. I imagine thats only a veeery small amount of them, but regardless you've given me a new pov to consider. Thank you!

OP youre framing this wrong. There are a lot of sins in the Bible. Lying, adultery, being disrespectful to your parents. But me personally, I dont hate these people at all. I think theyre just normal people since everyone is a sinner. Everyone sins. That doesnt mean Im liarphobic or I dont support liars to have rights in the world?
Its the same for homosexuality. I think that loving thy neighbour is much more important than judging gay people. I dont just tolerate them. I love them, if youre trans no problem. Lesbians i love you. Youre just a normal person who has committed a sin, but everyone in the world also has. But i believe they should have rights, should be free and live in a world free of discrimination.
Thats just my Christian beliefs. Since I believe in The Holy Trinity I cant completely actively change my beliefs, so if you dont support my beliefs, are you Christianphobic

Homophobia describes attitudes, behaviors, or beliefs that show dislike, hostility, prejudice, or fear toward homosexual people. Christianophobia is defined as an irrational hatred, fear, prejudice, or discrimination directed at Christians, their practices, beliefs, or symbols. If I disagree with your religious beliefs i'm not Christianophobic, but if you "disagree" with the identity of a gay person you are, in fact, homophobic.

U disagree with my Christian beliefs so arent you prejudicial towards how i feel? This feels like a clear hypocrisy. Alsp i dont disagree with their identity. I just think its a sin, but youre welcome to do whatever you want. I think everyone should br equal

No. prejudice is a preconceived opinion that is not based on reason or actual experience. I used to be christian, and most of my family is christian, so my opinion is heavily based on not only reason, but actual experience.

My reason is because Im christian. Is that not a good enough reason? I have a strong faith and belief in my religion which justifies my beliefs. Also if you think prejudice against gay people means even just believing they are sinners despite supporting their rights, then you think all kind of prejudice against gay people is homophobia.
So i think you should understand that prejudice isnt always negative. It can also be positive, so if I think all gay people are happy and kind, am i homophobic cuz I have a preconceived opinion not based on reason or experience

so if I think all gay people are happy and kind, am i homophobic cuz I have a preconceived opinion not based on reason or experience
I think you know that isn't the same

Yeah and i think you should know me thinking homosexual people are sinful isnt homophobic because everyone sins as well. You have a specific definition of it only to prove ur case.
Its not homophobic

A lot of tounge and cheek memes but also a lot of people serious that we're all going to die in a nuclear war or something like that in the next few years.
I think the first reason is that Iran, Ukraine etc are all proxy wars. Like we saw in the Cold War. The superpowers (and I include Europe as one) simply do not want to face each other head on. International precedents remain stronger than you think and even Trump is afraid of the consequences. Yes proxy wars can turn into something bigger but Russia has tested this in Ukraine and both sides seem pretty committed to keeping it contained despite rhetoric.
I think nuclear war is out of the question here to put it simply: MAD.
So with nukes out of the picture if a WW was to happen we need rearment. Yes Russia and China have huge armies, they always have done. The US have a large army but no way big enough for a Vietnam sized conflict let alone a WW. Europe are increasing size but too slowly and to too small a size to cause any alarm about imminent conflict or drafting etc. It's natural they we all want a slightly bigger army atm but this doesn't mean WW is to come, it's largely to keep people tf out their space.
The only way I see a WW is if it takes place through cyber attacks / economically. Which is not what social media are focusing on.
Anyway a lot more I have to say but I don't have all that time on my hands!

Its the axis of pedophiles vrs the axis or resistence to western imperialism and colonization. Every country is on a side, lebanon iran probably yemen is being bombed as well as saudi arabia, dubai, kuwait, israel. Its kind of a world war all thats not currently in it is europe but its just the first week and every serious person has always said invading iran would last much longer then iraq.
Well no. What about Canada, Japan, Russia, China, South East Asia, South America?

China and russia are helping iran afaik.
Also there have been 30 complaints this weekend throughout the US army and navy that commanders have told their soldiers that this is a war to cause armageddon to raise jesus
https://jonathanlarsen.substack.com/p/us-troops-were-told-iran-war-is-for?utm_source=share&utm_medium=android&r=2pxwt&triedRedirect=true
But to be a world war there would have to be boots on the ground. Not just intel or weapons, maybe that would be the case in the past but things are too complicated for that.

There is no opposition party we can just put in as a puppet. We already killed their leader and most of the top brass but there is no sign of stopping. If we don't retreat, but bomb themm into civil war to chop it up to Isis/Al quida CIA asssets like SYria then we will absolutely need to keep heavy surveillence and watch, any reconstruction contracts given to western companies or any involvement at all will be excused by resourse extraction, all of which require boots on the ground for oil securities. The only way we wouldn't have boots in the ground is if its just endless bombing like Gaza, and that is banking on a gamble that Iran and allies can't escalate things which is not a safe gamble at all.

No I'm saying boots on the ground from atleast China or Russia AND the US and maybe even Europe - who are involved in shooting one another.
I'm not sure having US soilders on a "peacemaking" mission will equate to WW3. Reminds me of Afghanistan.

Well Russia would say that the war they are currently in meatgrinder trenches on a static front is already against western imperialism and colonization. China is definitely on the other side even though they haven't put boots on the ground since 79 they are still going to be the largest superpower that is a threat to the west.

Aren't we talking about Iraq or not?
Russia say this however there have been multiple opportunities for escalation but nothing but rhetoric has come out from the Kremlin in 4 years.

Russia and Ukraine have been in ww1 style trenches on a static front using expired outdated weaponry against each other where 50-70% conscripted recruits dragged to the front in ICE like ops are expected to die or get maimed within hours of their first rotation. For the last like 4 years...
Yes that is true but you've been throwing out snippets to me with no coherent argument.

CMV: Framing abortion as “my body, my choice” was a strategic and moral mistake; it should have always been framed as a healthcare and public health issue I’m broadly pro safe and legal abortion, but I think the dominant pro-choice framing has done real harm, especially to vulnerable women. I’m open to having my view changed.
My core position: Abortion policy should be argued and justified primarily as a healthcare and harm-reduction issue, not as an expression of individual autonomy or personal desire.
Here’s why: 1. Public policy is about collective outcomes, not individual preferences “My body, my choice” is an individualistic moral claim. Public policy, however, exists to maximize population-level benefit and minimize harm. Laws are not built around personal slogans; they are built around measurable outcomes like mortality, morbidity, and access to care.
Rights-based framing collapses into belief wars Once abortion is framed as bodily autonomy, it invites an endless moral standoff: “My body, my choice” vs “What about the fetus?” At that point, the debate becomes philosophical and belief-based, not evidence-based. No amount of argument convinces someone who fundamentally disagrees on metaphysics. Healthcare framing avoids this trap.
The moral status of the fetus becomes irrelevant at the policy level Even if one believes the fetus has moral value, the data consistently show that banning abortion: Increases maternal mortality Increases unsafe abortions Does not meaningfully reduce abortion rates Policy should ask: Which approach saves more lives and reduces suffering? That answer is access to safe, legal, and regulated abortion, regardless of moral disagreement.
“My body, my choice” individualizes and moralizes blame This framing implies abortion is a free, empowering choice, when in reality, many abortions are: Traumatic Forced by poverty, coercion, or medical risk The least bad option among many bad options The slogan subtly shifts responsibility onto women, making it easier for society to judge them rather than address structural failures.
It alienates conservatives and fuels backlash Many activists come from socially liberal environments and rarely engage conservatives except antagonistically. Rights-based, confrontational rhetoric: Hardens opposition Turns abortion into a culture-war symbol Makes compromise and harm reduction politically impossible Healthcare framing lowers perceived threat and allows moderates to support policy even if they personally oppose abortion.
Vulnerable women pay the price for activist signaling The women most affected by abortion restrictions are not activists. They are often poor, young, religious, or socially constrained. Loud, identity-driven messaging increases stigma, guilt and shame, making abortion harder to seek quietly and safely. A healthcare approach would protect them better.
Activist framing has likely increased stigma, not reduced it By turning abortion into a moral and political identity, activism has: Increased polarization Made abortion more visible and moralized Strengthened punitive responses in conservative societies Stigma thrives in polarization.
Abortion should be treated like other harm-reduction healthcare Not celebrated. Not politicized. Handled quietly, clinically, compassionately, like emergency contraception, post-exposure prophylaxis, or mental health crisis care. Necessary, supported, and evidence-based.
Edit: I've been convinced that the current framing was born out of historical context.

Yeah my pro life morals aren’t going to be changed by some claims of a “greater good” in public health outcomes. If you believe abortion is murder, which I do, it’s like saying there’s great public health outcomes by killing the fat people. I agree that abortion activist rhetoric has hurt the abortion industry more than it has helped though.

You can be pro life, but data shows that stopping abortion does not lead to zero abortion, but causes unsafe abortions....
What we need is proper counselling and screening for women undergoing this...Abortion is often times most horrifying for the woman themselves..Yhe current framing just creates stigma...

I will add that we need a lot more resources for vulnerable women who are pregnant. That’s where I greatly diverge from your typical right wing pro life position. My faith has been pro life for 2000 years so I’m not coming at this from a modern political perspective. There’s many ways to improve public health outcomes, not just abortion. People can downvote me all they want but I’m giving you an honest answer to your proposition.

I do agree that a lot needs to be done for pregnant women besides abortion access...I can't argue on religious basis...I am a Christian myself and consider abortion to be permissible in Christianity, but interpretation can vary. I can't explain well how I reached my conclusion in religious context.
But that said would you change your stance based on what studies, evidence and data shows or not?

The first Christian catechism in the first century, explicitly prohibits abortion. Look up the Didache. That was the position of all Christian’s until the 20th century. I will not change my stance, no matter how many greater goods are claimed. Because we can always do more that’s not abortion.

I will agree that we can always do more than just abortion. Counselling, Screening and so much more is needed. But again the focuses should always be the womans life and health, followed by the fetus'...Not the other way around. I hope we can agree on that?

CMV: People who know they have chronic mental health conditions should feel an obligation to treat them when they learn they are going to become parents. I’ll start by pointing out the obvious: if you have a chronic mental health condition, then you might not be rational enough to know that you should treat it. I truly do get that. So, this will be the first of a few caveats: My claim in the title applies specifically to people who have chronic mental health conditions AND are rational enough to know they can treat it.
Caveat number 2: This claim is in no way an effort to say that people with chronic mental health conditions shouldn’t have children, or that they should be punished for becoming parents and not treating their conditions. Plenty of people with chronic mental health conditions are also excellent parents, especially if they are trying to actively treat them, and if they’re transparent with their children about their condition. And as far as punishment goes, this just seems wrong because there are plenty of chronic mental health conditions that do not put anybody in any serious danger, so I think that any punishment should be more associated with harmful actions or significant likelihood of them as opposed to just having a chronic mental illness.
That all being said, here’s why I think this is still a valid claim after adding the caveats: children see their parents as examples of how to live, and they see their parents that way both from the lessons their parents teach and from the behaviors they exhibit. If a parent has to flip a light switch 3 times in order to feel like the house won’t burn down, then child will think that’s what you have to do. This creates thought processes that will either stick unhealthily or that will have to be overcome in order to function later on in life. If you can’t personally overcome this sort of thing, then you should still feel a responsibility to treat it as best you can AND to let your child know that this is your thing that you know is unhealthy and that your child does not have to do the same thing. If you don’t do these things, then you are setting your child up to potentially either have or not know how to handle the same conditions.
There have been some studies done on people who are in longterm forced proximity with people who have chronic mental health conditions, such as children of parents with these conditions. The children frequently exhibit some of the same behaviors as their parents, but ultimately they aren’t actually diagnosed with the conditions themselves. They just have the behaviors they have because they’ve learned to do certain things in order to cope with the emotions that their parents express due to the anxiety their parents feel when they don’t do those behaviors. Using the light switch example, if a parent needs to flip a switch 3 times and the child doesn’t do that, then the parent may explode emotionally, so the child learns to flip the switch 3 times just to avoid that outburst, effectively “becoming” someone with the same conditions in the presence of their parents, and then in the rest of the world they sometimes have to fight that conditioning in order to function normally.
When you become a parent, your life is not about you anymore. If you are rational enough to know you have a chronic mental health condition and to know you can treat it, but you for some reason choose not to, then you’re doing a massive disservice to your children.

If it takes someone a year of mental health treatment to just become 30% less anxious about a light switch, then that doesn’t mean that the treatment is ineffective. That means that it’s working and needs to continue.
But if it's a choice between flipping a light switch a few times and not being anxious about it vs continuing to sink time and money into treatment and still being 70% anxious about it, might it be better to just accept flipping the light switch a few times? Sure, a kid might grow up with some weird habits around light switches, but having less access to a parent because they're in therapy a lot, less financial resources because the parents are spending money on therapy, and a patient who's more anxious than they could be otherwise is going to have consequences to the child too.

!delta
I think what we’re talking about is essentially the availability of the treatment itself, and whether being able to afford it and be present for it would significantly detract from the other things that a paren could do with that money and time. That is absolutely fair, and this is a system issue that one parent on an individual level can’t necessarily resolve.
I could see how there may be a situation where a kid would be better off with some odd habits learned from their parents if the alternative is a parent who can’t afford food and is around significantly less.

First, I appreciate the delta.
That is absolutely fair, and this is a system issue that one parent on an individual level can’t necessarily resolve.
The system issue applies to the financial constraints, but you can't change the system to make therapy not take time, and if anxiety is still higher after therapy than it is after flipping a light switch a few times, systems can't really fix that either.

Just for a time perspective, I have to say that I disagree. Let’s assume you have money for therapy, so therefore the cost is not the issue. There is always 30 minutes a week for therapy. If it’s important to you, then you can find the time. There are therapists that work well into the evening, and that don’t cost more. You can do it entirely online. People make time to see the dentist and the physician, and we make time for watching Netflix and going to the gym. If you can make a sacrifice for other health related things then you can also make time for your mental health, without it costing anything more than what it would cost for traditional in person local therapy.

My claim in the title applies specifically to people who have chronic mental health conditions AND are rational enough to know they can treat it.
I mean, it becomes difficult to then imagine a person who the view could possibly apply to. What rational person would be like "My crippling chronic depression which reasonably could be treated? Making things difficult for my kids? Nah... it's fine. I'll get around to it. No big deal"
The view is basically that everyone thinking irrationally should instead think rationally, with the caveat that you know irrational people can't think rationally so it doesn't apply to them. "I believe everyone must remember to take their umbrella with them on rainy days, except for the people who forget to."

That is absolutely a type of person. Maybe not the quote you said specifically, but the type of person who has something and doesn’t treat it? Yeah I mean I’d say that’s a large portion of Reddit itself.

Right but obviously those people are not making a rational choice. Most people are just irrational, it turns out. Anybody "rational enough to know they can treat it," would just obviously, you know, do that
Like you might as well have said "CMV: all people who are overweight, should just diet and exercise until they aren't anymore. Caveat: does not apply to people who are unwilling to diet or exercise."

I considered making this about physical conditions, too, but I felt as though a more focused point would be better because we can just talk about one thing. But yes, I’d agree with something to the effect of “CMV: Medically obese people should feel a responsibility to become physically healthy when they learn they’re going to become parents,” with a caveat of “this does not apply to people who are somehow too irrational to be aware of the fact that they’re obese.”
I would not include the caveat you said in your example regarding obesity. My caveat was about having the mental ability to be aware of what you have, not about having the personal will to change it.

I'm still kind of hung up on the hypothetical person rational enough to realize that treatment is both needed and effective, but for some other reason still refuses treatment. Is this hypothetical person just like, stupid? Are they dumb

You’ll have to ask people here on Reddit. I think there are a lot of reasons people don’t get treatment despite knowing they have something and can treat it. Sometimes it’s the attention they get for it, sometimes they’re able to use it as an excuse for their behavior, sometimes they think they’ll be worse at something without it (look at how many musicians think they won’t be good anymore if they treat their depression or kick an addiction). It’s different for almost everybody.
Surely every one of those falls under the umbrella of irrationality

I think it’s a spectrum, but if you know you have something and know you can treat it but still don’t, then by saying that you’re acknowledging that you’re rational enough to know that you can treat it. It’s just an additional layer of irrationality that says “but I won’t” for whatever reason that is to you.
So the view is just that people who know that they should do something, but haven't, should do it
Yeah but specifically because they find out they’re going to have kids, due to how they may treat their kids or what they might make their kids think is normal.

CMV: Democrats should run a real progressive in 2028 because any democratic candidate will be painted as an socialist immigrant-loving USA-hating demon by right-wing media. There's an argument around that democrats should run a centrist because if they run a progressive, they might scare off voters.
The problem with this argument is voters are going to be scared off no matter who you run. Fox News decides on a narrative and then runs with it, regardless of it's basis in reality, and it's always going to slur the democrat as an evil socialist. And to the viewers, it will be completely true.
Every day it STILL paints Joe Biden, one of the blandest, most establishment neo-liberals in history, as a progressive socialist demon who loves immigrants (despite deporting more immigrants his first year than Trump during the last year).
***
Progressive ideas are widely-popular. Do you want healthcare? Do you want more wages? Everybody wants those things. Everybody needs them now more than ever.
But the only way to get them is to run someone who actually believes in them and fights for them. Obama for all his talk was a neo-liberal centrist. His only real accomplishment for 8 years was the ACA, which was a watered-down version of a plan written by Mitt Romney, a republican. Universal Healthcare didn't happen at a time when the country was ready for it because Obama didn't really believe in it and didn't fight tooth and nail for it.
2028 may be the one and only chance to get a real progressive in the White House. The political pendulum has swung so far right we're about to implode as a country- everyone knows we have to go left. Whoever the Dems run are going to be painted as far-left to scare voters- they might as well actually be far-left and get some shit done because it's not fun and games anymore- the country needs real big changes.

CMV: The "cow" punishment ("gas chamber") said to have been used on some captured Nazis in postwar Eastern Europe, wasn't actually that harsh or effective. I've come across stories of this punishment in a few places and it's been bothering me that it wasn't that effective a form of justice.
Note that I am not debating if these stories are real or not, even though I know that's sometimes a point of contention - I've been convinced that they are (did some independent research and asked around some Polish/ Eastern Europe FB groups etc and it seems some people have heard of this, even if most had not), but even if they're not, I guess my CMV would become "the punishment wouldn't have been effective if it had been used").
From what I had read, in some places after the war, german soldiers would be stuck in Eastern Europe, afraid to come home for fear of getting caught. Many tried to blend in and live normal lives discretely and this went on for many years.
By the 50s, some jurisdictions had banned any criminal prosecutions when said Nazis were caught, but there was such a strong sense of justice among the local populace that they had to suffer for what they'd done, that authorities would turn a blind eye to their punishment at the hand of local "citizen groups", as long as they would not be killed or maimed or seriously injured.
This necessitated the locals sometimes having to get creative with their punishment.
One I read of was the "cow". The Nazi would be taken to a barn, made to stand behind a cow, nose 3 cm from the cow's butt. Hands cuffed or tied behind back, legs shackled, neck put in a restraint/yoke to prevent movement.
12 hours a day they'd be locked in this position on the cow. The other 12 they'd be taken off and locked in a stall in the barn to recover / eat / sleep / reflect.
20 days (of 12 hours a day) was a typical sentence, with a reduction of a few hours for every other stowaway Nazi the offender could rat out.
One elderly Pole had commented to point out that from what they were told long ago, the punishment wasn't invented from scratch but actually inspired by a much older Cavalry form of discipline. And also by the "gas chamber" pun - "you led the innocent to gas chambers, now you get a 'gas' chamber" I guess is the idea.
I remember seeing something where everyone was saying how incredible this idea is, how it's such a fitting form of justice, how we need that here for Nazis, etc.
Which is what surprised me and the source of my CMV: I don't see this punishment would actually be that effective or bad.
Sue it's gross, but so what? I assume you'd get used to the smell over time. Yes the cow would poo, maybe once an hour as a worst-case scenario, but I assume you'd just hold your breath for those events and the worst of it would fall off you.
Also I once had to pick up cow dung from a pasture, and it's just...not that bad? Farm kids would pick it up with bare hands even sometimes.
I just don't see what about this was said to be the devastating, unbearable-yet-physically-harmless punishment that others were saying it was. Or how a hardened war criminal could fear it.
Wouldn't 99% of it just be standing there kinda bored behind a cow? 1% is pretty gross but...it's just gross, and that's all?
Maybe I'm missing something from having never been to a dairy or large cow barn, but I feel like I would laugh and breathe a sigh of relief if a tribunal captured me for serious offenses and I learned 20 days of this was the extend of my punishment.
But for the sake of Schadenfreude and justice I kinda hope I'm wrong.
Would this be worse than I'm thinking? CMV
Physical exhaustion paired with methane inhalation (which is not inherently a problem, but displaces oxygen)

Cows digestive systems work quite differently from our own, because they are ruminants.
Cows actually burp far more regularly than they fart, approximately once every 90 seconds, and its through this process that they vent the bulk of their digestive gasses.
But cows do fart, roughly once every 10 minutes or so, and this is the means by which they expell excess humidity from their digestive tracts, which run quite hot compared to those of humans.
So, if you can picture it, restrained with the arms behind the back and over the head (effectively pole cricifixion) already making breathing difficult, every 10 minutes comes a burst of dense humidity, methane, and stinking particulate matter, exiting the cows ass at a temperature well in excess of 125 degrees. It would be agonizing. The inability to get sufficint oxygen from the posture is already enough to cause severe anxiety and disorientation. The cow farts mostly would just punctuate this state with something to panic about.

CMV: Billionaires don't believe in democracy and it is ethical and pro-democratic to set up guardrails against people obtaining that level of wealth I am generally pro-capitalist and I believe that a balanced economic structure will have some variant of capitalism with most of the incentive structures capitalism produces. I believe it is good to reward people for their hard work and or ingenuity by allowing them to live their best lives with the money they've earned. But there is a difference between live-your-best-life wealth and control-the-world wealth. I don't claim to know where that cutoff happens but assuredly by the time someone's net worth is on the order of a billion dollars of today's money, they are dealing in control-the-world wealth.
Billionaires will exercise their money as power by buying favors from government officials, through forming massive integrated conglomerates, and through investing their wealth in technology that will increase their power level. All of these run antithetical to the idea that power ultimately rests in the people and that each person has an equal say, which is the central tenet of democracy. By using their massive wealth to consolidate power, billionaires telegraph that they believe they deserve more of a say than the average person, which is not something you should be able to buy in a democracy.
We can debate the idea that capitalism is "fair" and whether billionaires are fairly rewarded based on their hard work and ingenuity (even though I think that's ridiculous), but I don't think it matters. Obtaining enough wealth to control the world is an abuse of the social contract and should not be entertained as a reasonable goal of hard work and ingenuity.

I agree with just about everything you said, but I want to point out that Democracy doesn't necessarily give equal power to each person.
The benefit of Democracy is that it stratifies power which is a good check against corruption as it is more difficult to bribe one thousand people than it is to bribe one. But not everyone needs to have voting power to get the benefits of stratification, just enough people.
The other benefit of Democracy is that it allows for the peaceful transition of power from one regime to the next. All power fundamentally comes from the capacity to do violence. Majorities win elections because the alternative is the majority rebels and kicks out the ruling party. The side with more people is more likely to win that potential conflict, so it is better for everyone if we just accept that fact, and use elections as a proxy for that rebellion and peacefully transition. But not everyone needs to have voting power to approximate a hypothetical rebellion, just the people with the greatest capacity for violence.
Athens was a Democracy, Rome was a Democracy, Antebellum America was a democracy, but none of those places had anything close to Universal Suffrage. There were literally slaves with few/no rights in those times and places who obviously didn't have equal power. But those places were still Democracies and had the benefits (and drawbacks) of Democracy.

CMV: Physician Assisted Suicide should be legal and normalized I'm fully aware I'm emotional about this right now, for the record
My dad is dying. He has been for a while. Diagnosed 3 years ago, given 6 months. Radiation bought him time, but almost killed him. It was undetectable December 2024. By Sept 2025, it was back with a vengeance. He called it. He couldn't take more radiation.
He's been on home hospice since mid January. His nurses thought he would be gone weeks ago, to the point I talked to him what I thought would be one last time first week of February. But he's been keeping going. He was able to communicate (he can barely talk) that he was hanging on so mom would get one more social security check.
But once March came, and mom told him it was the first, his blood pressure started to drop. He managed to communicate today that he felt it was coming, and asked Mom to call me. He couldn't do any more than groan. It's spread to the nodes in his neck so now his entire neck and face are swollen.
Dad was always a prideful man. He valued his independence and dignity above all. He took pride in being a provider and a caregiver.
Now he is being taken of by mom and her sisters (I'm 3000 miles away and cannot afford the time off nor travel expenses, which is killing me....). Naked, with a Foley catheter, wasting away, having to take medicine rectally because the cancer has completely sealed off his stomach.
Why?
When our beloved pets are sick, and there is no hope, we take them to the vet before life becomes a painful, miserable, hopeless and seemingly endless struggle....give them a little pin prick shot....and let them pass in our arms, comfortablely. Sometimes we can even do it in our own home. I've had a dog that we did that with. She laid in her own bed, being pet and cuddled, and had the people that loved her with her as her pain was taken away and given to those that would feel it for her.
Why don't we do that for our parents? For our loved ones? Why is there this insistence to extend life far beyond what anyone would consider livable? Why must we, for the sake of those that don't find it palatable, put our families in insurmountable debt trying to make us comfortable in our final weeks?
I genuinely don't understand how anyone would have an issue with it? Can anyone make me understand?

Okay so first, I’m sorry for you. I know how hard it is to watch someone suffer and not be able to do anything about it.
Now, there are many reasons people are against assisted euthanasia. But if we’re talking purely about policy and politics, one reason some liberal groups oppose legalization is that it premature. Without a rock solid social security it become a slippery slope where the most vulnerable among us are quietly pushed toward suicide, which would be a legalized state murder instead of help.
Just look at the USA. It’s a first world country where people are notoriously afraid to call an ambulance or go to the hospital because of the cost. Whether every story is true isn’t even the main point. The implication is. If a healthcare system can be designed in a way to pressures the poor and middle class to not use it, euthanasia law can and would be designed so that the same person would pursue it even when Good alternative exist in such system.
The people who would qualify for euthanasia are often those who can’t work for themselves, who are socially dependent, or were already vulnerable to begin with.
Without stronger social security, legalizing euthanasia too early would make it the obvious solution for the poorest. That would end up punishing and isolating those who still want to keep living but feel like a burden.

Yes, I can see that argument. My dads been cheating death for 25 years. The first time he got sick, he refused to go to the hospital for weeks. When we finally got him to go, the doctors had no idea how he was alive. He had suffered a heart attack and was septic.
That hospital stay cost my parents $250,000, my dad his job, and their standard of living. They had to move across the country to a family owned house, the debt was insurmountable.
In this insane world we have made, and are making, I can definitely see the fear that in that situation, if it were legal they mightve pressured him to just die.

My god! Your father rocks and this system suck! A quarter million! It's insane. But also is cheating death for a quarter century. Anyway I hope you all receive the solace you need. On the other hand, did I change your view?

Firstly, sincerest apologies for what you and your family are experiencing. And to your credit, this is the quintessential situation for when PAS should absolutely be legal.
That all said, we need to be very careful on when we decide it’s ok. The netherlands has a framework that I think works really well (last I checked), but it excludes patients who aren’t suffering from a terminal illness (so while your father absolutely would qualify, conditions like depression don’t). PAS should be legal, but not as broadly as a lot of PAS supporters tend to think

Back to the subject at hand though, I think having it be a part of advance directives would be the best course of action. Have a list of set conditions in which the patient has given the go ahead.

You’re exactly right on the advanced directives piece, that’s one way the netherlands allows for patient autonomy to be expressed, and even in the US that’s part of why it’s so widely encouraged. CMV: Asker culture is better than guesser culture Background: asker vs guesser is the observation that some people are raised asking for what they want and are happy with whatever answer they get, while others are raised to only ask for things if they have solid reason to believe the answer will be "yes." It's broader than that, but that's the gist.
Most sources discussing "Asker" vs "Guesser" culture take an overtly neutral stance, suggesting that neither is better than the other.
I disagree. In my experience, far more misery comes from guesser behavior than asker behavior, most of which is self-inflicted. Guessers exclude themselves from things they want, deny their own needs, and otherwise put themselves into compromising positions in order to protect themselves from hearing "no". They will get mad at askers around them, treating them as if they're OBVIOUSLY rude.
Askers, on the other hand, are more resilient in the face of unexpected answers to their questions, do not get offended as easily, and generally live a much more peaceful life with less stress. The only time asker behavior causes misery is when they interact with a guesser, or they are actually a bad person asking for bad things.
I believe asker is better than guesser both on an individual basis, and on a cultural basis. Societies that have a bunch of implied social rules making people feel bad for asking for what they want out of life are worse than societies that just say what they want and accept what comes.
I believe that guessers' lives would be better off if they learned to act like askers.
But these conclusions are all based on personal experience, and my own feelings during asker/guesser conflicts. If there is merit to the guesser point of view, I'd rather have a more realistic take.
So change my view...

Guess culture trains empathy.
Put yourself in the other person's shoes, and have some idea of how appropriate the request is. Before even asking.

I guess by that token, asker culture is friendlier to narcissists and sociopaths.

I think it's the opposite. Communications in "ask" culture contain very little information about the actual value functions and value judgements of the speaker. There is only the reified query. Communications in "guess" culture are much more rich and provide loads of contextual information about the preferences of everyone who is involved.
The only time asker behavior causes misery is when they interact with a guesser, or they are actually a bad person asking for bad things.
...or, importantly, when everyone says "no" to a thing you actually really need. Asker culture can leave the askers up shit creek without a paddle because everyone says "no" when you ask to borrow their paddle because no one wants shit on their paddle.

Why is "loads of contextual information about the preferences of everyone who is involved" preferable to explicit communication of those preferences by those people?
...or, importantly, when everyone says "no" to a thing you actually really need. Asker culture can leave the askers up shit creek without a paddle because everyone says "no" when you ask to borrow their paddle because no one wants shit on their paddle.
I don't understand how a guesser would be any better off in this scenario. The guesser never would have asked if they thought the answer was no, so the outcome is still no one shitting on their paddle.

I don't understand how a guesser would be any better off in this scenario. The guesser never would have asked if they thought the answer was no, so the outcome is still no one shitting on their paddle.
The key thing to realize is that the norm in guess-culture that the answer to a request be "yes" is on both the asker and the respondent to uphold. Asking someone more directly in guess-culture places more social pressure on the respondent to say "yes" in order to uphold that norm. This pressure can result in you getting a paddle when you need it.

In guesser culture, guessers have the ability to expect a yes to difficult questions.
In asker culture, askers have the ability to communicate persuasively and directly in order to persuade someone to say yes.
I do not believe either asker or guesser has an inherent advantage in this scenario as far as the ability to achieve the desired outcome.
The advantage here is the psychological impact. The guesser making the request will be a lot more pre-occupied and uneasy about asking in the first place, and the guesser answering the request will have the burden of needing to say yes to something they don't want to. All of this happens under the hood without the ability to negotiate or communicate an actual understanding of each participant's preferences. Edit: and the guesser will ruminate about requests they've made and answers they've received in ways that an asker will not.
In the asker case, by default there is no trepidation about asking or answering. If you want to persuade someone to say yes, then you give them more information about why you're asking and the impact of their answer so they can make an informed decision. They may still feel uneasy because of those emotional pleas, but that's inherent to the interpersonal relationship and is the point of persuasion.

In asker culture, askers have the ability to communicate persuasively and directly in order to persuade someone to say yes.
Guessers have this same ability. Nothing about guess culture stops you from telling someone else about your situation. In fact this is pretty much the default mode of communication in guess culture: telling someone what is happening in the hopes that they will freely offer to help.

You're making a number of points here, and I appreciate the discussion. First, when a citizen engages in prior violent criminal conduct --as Pretti did by kicking the vehicle after screaming profanities at the officers -- he will certainly be on their radar as a person who is capable of violence against the officers. Now, against this backdrop, Pretti blocked traffic, interfered with officers trying to effectuate an arrest--committing another felony-- and continued to fight them with a loaded gun on his person. Given his past and current conduct, the moment that the officer screamed "he's got a gun!", one could reasonably believe, based on his prior violent conduct and current unlawful conduct, that he was capable of gravely harming them. And this happened in a matter of second. So, when you look at the totality of the circumstances, I conclude that the shooting was a mistake and should not have occurred, but that the mistake was objectively reasonable under the circumstances, thus preluding prosecution. At bottom, Pretti's actions were unlawful and reckless--don't block traffic, interfere with an arrest, and fight the officers while you have a loaded gone in a climate that is already incredibly precarious because the mayor and cover refused to provide police protection for the ICE officers.

Could they have exercised better judgment? Yes. Was it murder? No.
Your statement that he did not pose any threat of death it irrelevant. What is relevant is what the officers perceived and reasonably believed in the span of three or four seconds. And again, his prior behavior and current unlawful conduct, coupled with "he's got a gun!" could lead officer to perceive a grave threat.
They didn't send out "masked men" to inflame the situation. Mayr Frey and Governor Walz inflamed the situation when they refused to provide police protection for the ICE officers, and when they refused to allow ICE to detain illegal aliens when they were about to be released from prison. Had they cooperated -- like nearly every jurisdiction does -- none of this would have happened. CMV: The number of nuclear armed countries is going to at least double in the next 20 years I think that the number of countries with nuclear capabilities is going to at least double in the next two decades and I think that this will usher in an era of geopolitical instability which the world has not seen since the 1940s. I am making this claim for the following two reasons-
The United States is stepping away from its role as guarantor of global security:
Since the end of World War II, the U.S. has stood as the first line of the defense in Europe and East Asia against Russian/Soviet and Chinese aggression. It has signaled that it will no longer do so. As such, there is very little reason for a powerful and technologically sophisticated power such as Germany or Japan not to pursue the ultimate chess piece. If either country acquired nuclear weapons, there would be little reason to dissuade a Poland or a South Korea — countries with profoundly traumatic experiences with the German and Japanese militaries — from obtaining their own nuclear arsenals.
2. The United States has become an agent of chaos:
This is kind of a corollary to point one but it deserves to be examined on its own. In stark contrast to the recent past, the United States has become very unpredictable and unruly. One could argue that this trend started with Iraq in 2003 but it is clear that its behavior has recently become more and more erratic. Actions like decapitation strikes of foreign leaders and casual threats to annex territories unilaterally make it clear that it’s close to impossible to predict the future behavior of the world’s most powerful military.
Taken together, these two developments make it almost unwise NOT to develop nuclear weapons.
If Iran or Venezuela had possessed nuclear weapons, it is certain their leaders would not have faced assassination or forcible removal.
If Russia did not possess nuclear weapons, it is plausible that Vladimir Putin might now be facing trial in The Hague or have been the target of a decapitation strike.
If Germany were to acquire nuclear weapons, it would gain the strategic autonomy to shape European security on its own terms.
If Turkey or Saudi Arabia were to acquire nuclear weapons, they would be able to negotiate with Israel and Iran as equals, with or without American backing.
For these reasons, I struggle to see why a rational leader in today’s environment would choose not to pursue nuclear weapons if given the opportunity.

CMV: Free Will cannot exist. There are several logical constraints to the existence of free will.
First of all, our acting by our free agency inevitably comes down to external circumstances animating us.
Decisions and choices are reactions to external events obviously out of one's control, and so our choice possibilities are shaped for us, and no choice could exist in a vacuum, as it is an interaction with our environment.
So, not only are our choices limited and defined by what happens external to ourselves, it is also impossible NOT to react or have a choice for any intelligent human being, so you are forced to act on circumstances and in ways you have little control over.
You are not acting based on an internal independent component of yourself, such as preferences, intelligence, whim, knowledge, etc. but are acting based on CIRCUMSTANCES interacting with, and shaping, those components.
You might recall a regretful decision you made 10 years ago. in a sense, you had acted based on your knowledge which translates to desire and then choice, and you did not know any better. but you know now that if an external circumstance were to shift and change your knowledge with it for the better, your preference would shift with it and so would your choice.
The internal drive that results in a certain choice is at the mercy of what happens outside of us.
You might call your ability to pick between a binary choice whose content is determined for you a manifestation of your free will, and so let's transition to the problem of agency itself.
Your agency, or Will, is also something you had little involvement in the process of shaping and differentiating from other humans' agencies.
When you pick X over Y, unlike your friend who picked Y, this you would say is based on your consistent personality, knowledge and individual preferences, but have you picked or willed any of these parameters? of course not.
As we had discussed, knowledge is shaped by what happens outside of our control and in accordance with the other components which produce a certain choice. and preference or desire is a pre-requisite to will and is a blind-spot of control no matter if its malleable or situation-based or consistent through out life and immutable.
You might say, as a counter-argument, that you had no choice over these but you had the choice to act based on them or not, and that in any case your will is free and independent of them.
Yet, for intelligent humans, all choices are biases, and stem from a preference, one way or another, the preference or desire to act based on or against our knowledge or other circumstances, or to not act at all. and would you say this final preference to act on preference or other parameters was willed or chosen by you? it could be the fundamental expression of your a priori core self, but did you ever get to choose to be you, over anybody else?
Will could be entirely independent the past, you would say, any baggage internal or external, only a pure desire born out of the moment, and that is where its freedom lies. if it is not dependent on who you are, nor on any reason but merely chooses to be reasonable or not based on... what? nothing? an unknown purpose? this supernatural force is still constrained by the logically necessary dichotomy of being either: random (thus irrational, not free, out of anything's control) or consistent (thus determined, not free).
Free will seems impossible to me.
TL;DR the possibilities of choices your will makes are determined for it, if your will is reasonable and interacts with what is external and based on knowledge or preferences, then it is shaped and determined by out-of-control factors, and if your will can choose whether to act on knowledge and preference, it is still a preference and if this preference is entirely free of circumstance internal or external and merely chooses whether or not to act based on them it either has to be a random, thus not free, or consistent, thus determined.

You've argued quite well that Libertarian Free Will, wherein you are the cause of your actions but your actions aren't caused by you - the type of Free Will that theologians tend to like - doesn't make sense.
But that's not what most people mean most of the time when they talk about people acting of their own free will. Instead, people are generally referring to acting on the basis of who you are, in a manner sufficiently lacking in coercion that the action you took was determined by you (the person that exists) rather than by the coercing force.
That sort of free will does exist, we can point to people who are engaging in actions of their own free will.
Now, you COULD choose to declare that that doesn't count as free will - but from my point of view that's like saying: "Ponies aren't real. Ponies come in three breeds of intelligent talking creature, having earth-based powers, wings, or unicorn-horns and no real animal matches any of those. The animals that people point to and call ponies are just mundane animals, so they're not really ponies."
You're 100% right that the thing you're talking about doesn't exist - but you're wrong to think that that means that "free will" doesn't exist.

Likewise, I am responsible for my actions.

I am arguing that humans are no different than a bomb in the very latter highlighted part of this.

Okay, so lets be clear - is your position that moral responsibility REQUIRES that the entity responsible have created itself?
That someone can't have moral responsibility for something that you merely chose to do of your own free will, you have to have chosen to choose to choose to choose to choose to choose to choose to choose.... etc. it or it doesn't count?

The problem is not creating or causing yourself, the problem is that if, as we agreed, our choices are determined by who we are, how can your will be free if it responds out of a consistent, thus a pre-set and determined, Self? it would mean that you could not have acted otherwise since you choices are your own, you have a consistent, intentional self that determines actions. otherwise, it would be random.
Even if we put a causal point of view aside which falls into infinite regression, adopting your static argument which you posited in your original comment of having a set, defined self with specific relations to every other input, such as the external reality you deal with, you have a set response born of being yourself, it "reveals you", your choices cannot have been otherwise in this case, so there is no moral responsibility, and moral blame would be equivalent to blaming a programmed robot or an animal, which we clearly see as could not have helped being itself. and since we agreed our actions are determined by who we are, and who we are is an immutable parameter, it follows that we are quite the same.

By using a definition of "free will" that matches with normal usage rather than the libertarian version that is inherently self-contradictory.
 your choices cannot have been otherwise in this case
Ah, this is a common mistake - you use "cannot", but that's not true. They could have been different, if circumstances had been different or the person had been different. They WOULD NOT have been different (with no changes), but the word "can" is about counterfactuals, not about the deterministic course of events.
moral blame would be equivalent to blaming a programmed robot or an animal
Something that any sensible person would do, yes. If a robot killed someone because of its programming, we wouldn't go "go free robot, you didn't acausally cause yourself to be a murderbot" - we would shut it down. When an animal kills someone, we put it down.

CMV: The fact that the two most recent recent special elections in Texas went to Democrats indicates that the country is rejecting extreme MAGA-ism as a whole In the January 31, 2026 special elections, Democrats not only secured the U.S. House seat in Texas’s 18th Congressional District with Christian Menefee winning the runoff by a large margin, narrowing the Republican majority in the House but also flipped a Texas State Senate seat long held by Republicans. Keep in mind, this was a district Donald Trump carried by about 17 points in 2024. This swing of over 30 points relative to Trump’s performance strongly suggests voters are willing to break with GOP-aligned candidates in traditionally red territory.
Combined with national analysis showing Democrats outperforming expectations in other off-year and special elections, these results feel like more than isolated local quirks.
Of course, special elections are imperfect predictors and I acknowledge that low turnout and unique local factors that don’t always translate to general elections are certainly a consideration. Also, in some cases structural advantages like gerrymandered districts and geographic polarization still shape outcomes - but in Texas this is very much mitigated by their legislative ability to manage voter district control.
All this being said, the magnitude of the swing in a district Trump won handily, combined with Republican officials openly framing the results as a “wake-up call” and Democratic strategists pointing to a pattern of over-performance, makes it more than reasonable to argue that voters are growing tired of extreme MAGA rhetoric and are increasingly willing to punish it at the ballot box.
CMV.

CMV: Not supporting a cause does not automatically make you an enemy of it I noticed mostly in the mainstream media today a greater emergence in this mentality of "if you're not with us, you're against us." That if you do not support a particular group, cause, lifestyle etc then you are automatically deemed to be in opposition to it. This is prevalent in all sides of the divide across all manner of issues.
The truth is, the same freedom that allows you to have an opinion or fight for a cause gives another person the right to choose to not support it or to respectfully disagree. To me personally, a person is only an "enemy" of a cause when they take active steps to prevent others from supporting it too.
I'm not a religious person but I belong to a particular faith. So automatically, I would not subscribe to any other faiths. However, that does not mean I believe those faiths to be my enemy or that I myself must actively oppose those who follow other religions. Same way that if I were a vegan, I would not consider those who eat meat as my enemy so long as they don't force me to eat meat against my will.
There's nothing wrong with saying "I don't agree with you but I respect your right to feel that way, subscribe to this view or practice this lifestyle." Polite disagreements do not make people bigots or unpatriotic or enemies of the cause.
In today's time, we should learn to disagree better rather than expect total support and be disappointed when others have differing viewpoints.

CMV: Someone disagreeing with you, or pointing out inaccuracies in what you say, are not things that result in you “not being able to say anything anymore.” I’m not talking from a legal perspective. I’m not a lawyer. I also don’t know what it’s like living in a country that doesn’t have freedom of speech as an engrained liberty. I could be hyperbolic and say that “we’re losing our freedom of speech here!” in my country, but not only do I not believe that to be the case in my country, but I also, to reiterate, am not saying what I’m saying from a legal perspective.
When you’re in a conversation with any amount of regular, non-government-agent human beings, and one of those people points out inaccuracies in what you’re saying, disagrees with you, lets you know that you’re mistaken, or otherwise engages with you in such a way that makes it clear that their goal is to let you know they see differently from you about what you said, this is not a violation of your ability to speak.
You have the ability to walk away or continue the conversation, but if you think a good response is “I’m not allowed to say anything anymore,” yes you are. Nobody can ever stop you from saying anything.
If murderbots roamed the streets and constantly blared “no talking,” you still have the ability to talk. The only thing that can stop you from talking is some sort of surgery or injury that actually deducts from your body the means to communicate.
And I’m not trying to be ultra literal here. I know when most people say “you can’t say anything anymore,” they’re just frustrated and they don’t think someone is actually trying to stop them biologically from communicating. But I do think that there’s some part of them somewhere inside of them that thinks that being told they can’t do something actually makes it the case.
If I go to a bar and I’m talking with some friends, and someone around me who I don’t know tells me to shut up, I don’t have to do so. That person has done nothing to prevent me from speaking. That person just doesn’t want me to speak. I can still do so. There’s a bit more pressure on me to maybe be quiet as opposed to speaking, but that pressure isn’t a requirement. If I choose not to speak due to that person, that person didn’t make me stop speaking. I chose to stop speaking. I did to myself what I pictured that person wanted to make me do, but that didn’t.
I’m using this example because if telling someone to be quiet isn’t actually an obligation to be quiet, then disagreeing with someone and expressing that isn’t either. And this isn’t a “freedom of speech, not freedom from consequences” thing, either. It’s very simple: nobody can take away your ability to speak but you, and people using physical force. That’s it. Nothing else.

When someone says they “can’t say anything anymore”, they don’t mean they physically cannot.
There is an implied “without someone saying something that makes me feel annoyed or uncomfortable” after the statement.
Or more simply “I can’t do this thing without consequences”

!delta
That’s what I’m missing. So they’re kind of just leaving out the next part. Thank you.

CMV: Most of the far right-wing talking points demonizing migrants ironically apply more to billionaires CMV: Many far-right talking points demonizing migrants like welfare dependency, criminality, tax evasion, cultural threat, you name it, apply far more accurately to the ultrawealthy. In light of the Epstein files, it becomes clear that elites embrace the vices and behaviors they project onto migrants through their media influence and lobbied politicians. My point is - the criticisms leveraged against migrants are often literally embodied by the ultrawealthy.
1) Migrants are welfare queens <-> Ultrawealthy parasitism
Migrants are net contributors, while the ultrawealthy massively exploit tax breaks and government subsidies.
2) Migrants are criminals <-> Ultrawealthy are criminals
Migrants perform less crime than natives on average, while the ultrawealthy are notorious rapists, scammers, fraudsters, abusers, exploiters. Granted, the types of crime change, but they only change in scale and complexity.
3) Migrants don't pay taxes <-> Ultrawealthy tax evasion
Migrants do pay their fair share of taxes, while the ultrawealthy do everything to avoid them by storing it in wealth, stocks and creative accounting, often allowed by taxing rules they lobbied for.
4) Migrants don't integrate <-> Ultrawealthy form insular elite networks
Unless barred systematically or economically, migrants have little difficulty integrating into society. On the other hand, the rich are insular, forming parallel societies that considers itself superior to everyone else. Private schools, gated communities, global networks and socializing away from society on degenerate decadent parties (Including Epstein island), insulating themselves far away from social accountability.
5) Migrants are a cultural threat <-> Ultrawealthy erode culture
Migrants are told to erode the culture they inhabit. By ownership or direct influence on most media and social media, it is the ultrawealthy that do so - shaping people's tastes, opinions on any subject, influencing their political opinions, all the way down to fashion and product tastes.
6) Migrants are a security threat <-> Ultrawealthy are a geopolitical threat
Migrants are said to be an unsafe influence or presence, be it due to their suspected criminality or unsavory world views. Meanwhile, the ultrawealthy manipulate markets, lobby for conflicts and influence international policy for personal profit.
7) Migrants are lazy or unambitious <-> Ultrawealthy exploit labor
Migrants are said to be unproductive, but in fact they work essential and often underpaid jobs. Meanwhile, the ultrawealthy sit on money-making stock or other wealth, delegating most work to others or taking credit for their achievements.
8) Migrants take jobs <-> Ultrawealthy exploit labor laws and push for AI
Migrants don't actually take your job, often they fill an employment gap that otherwise wouldn't be filled. On the other hand, the ultrawealthy engage in union busting and work on reducing the amount of labor their wealth generation requires, potentially costing an average person's job.
9) Migrants are immoral or lack moral values <-> Ultrawealthy embrace decadence
Migrants are not any more or less moral than any other person. Meanwhile, I don't think I need to cite further than the Epstein files to show the ultrawealthy engage in all manner of immoral activity, ranging from financial fraud, sex trafficking networks, pedophilia, hell, there's even disturbing allegations of engaging in cannibalism.
10) Migrants influence elections <-> Ultrawealthy control political agendas
Migrants are accused of introducing or supporting foreign or threatening political ideology. Meanwhile, the ultrawealthy spend millions directly or indirectly to support candidates that supports their agenda, while marginalizing anyone not in their sphere of influence. Nothing more undemocratic.
I think if you go on, you can find more juxtapositions. CMV.
Edit: I want to reiterate that this isn’t about individuals or partisan politics, but about an ironic structural pattern. You will always find cases that confirm or contradict stereotypes. The point is that the behaviors often criticized in migrants tend to apply more to the ultrawealthy.
Edit2: It's true that the anti-immigrant talking points do not stem solely from the right wing and make no claim that it does, but I think it's safe to say that's where it currently stems from.
I intentionally made no distinction between immigrants and illegal immigrants as while anti-immigrant narratives tend to target exclusively illegal immigrants, legal immigrants tend to be targetted by the resulting negative sterotypes and narratives nonetheless.

Even if some elites dodge taxes or commit crimes, it doesn’t follow that migrant concerns are "really" about billionaires. Migration raises distinct issues, border capacity, wage competition in some sectors, housing/service strain, and integration frictions, none of which billionaire misconduct explains away. Two problems can coexist, swapping targets is false equivalence/whataboutism.

True, not all concerns with the migrants find a parallel in the ultrawealthy.
However when they do coincide, the criticisms repeatedly leveled at migrants often describe behaviors disproportionately exhibited by the ultrawealthy, yet get applied to them dispropoprtionely less.

Even where parallels exist, "who does it more" doesn’t make the migrant critique wrong or "ironic." Different groups affect the public in different ways: billionaire tax abuse is diffuse and policy mediated, migration impacts are local, immediate, and visible (schools, housing, jobs). People focus on salient, proximate effects, not because elites are innocent, but because the mechanisms differ.

!delta
Correct, I did not mean to dismiss valid critique. I mainly wanted to highlight the disproportionate narrative attention.
My perception was never that the mechanisms themselves are more immediately perceivable, for example compared to wealthy tax evasion (even if statistically migrants are net contributors while tax avoidance has more disastrous effects). Rather, my point was that the negative narratives are amplified by the ultrawealthy and they benefit from someone else being the scapegoat. I concede that I did not fully consider how the mechanics themselves may contribute to the narrative’s disproportion.

The top 1% pay 40% of all personal income tax collected in the USA. The bottom 50% pay 3% of all personal income tax collected in the USA.

No amount of tax or property confiscation will be enough to placate some people's envy or sense of entitlement, as long as there are others with more.
No amount of money or exploitation will be enough to placate some people's greed or lust for power.

That may be true for some. But try to avoid falling into the common envy trap of thinking other people's success and social status are due exclusively to their character flaws.
"I have more status than THOSE people down there because I work hard and am talented. But those other people up there only got there because of greed and exploitation".

No, I do not deny some success is legitimate.

OK I'm agreeing with you more now.

On whole, prosperous and predictable economic times generally benefit billionaires more than crises.

It's not just stifling competition.
Stable times reward people who are actually building things, sure, but crises tend to reward the people who already have a lot of capital and liquidity. They can buy up distressed assets, increase their ownership share and come out of the crisis controlling more of the market than before.
I’m not saying that’s immoral - that’s how markets work. My concern is what happens over repeated cycles. If every downturn increases ownership concentration, then wealth growth becomes less about continued contribution and more about who already owns the most. That’s where the structural imbalance starts to mirror a feudal oligarchy, not a democracy.

CMV: It is very dumb that some professional sports leagues, such as the NFL and NBA, hand their championship trophy to an executive.
