CMV: Changing the education system in the US will not change much. The US has an education problem, that is undeniable. But the issue is not that our education system is bad, it's really not. The issue is that our culture doesn't seem to value education very much. I lived outside of the US for 4 years during middle and high school, and other countries seem to have a lot more aspiring students who value their education as more than just a career spring board. We've been telling children they need to get through school, get a degree, and start working for so long it's no wonder they did the bare minimum in school while retaining nothing.
TL:DR People in the US tend not to value education for educations sake, and the solution to that is a cultural change not an education system change.

Your own experience might have involved a solid education when you were in the US; mine did too. But the US education system is patchy - there isn't actually "a" system - and large swaths of it are bad. What you point to is a real problem, but we're also increasingly graduating people who, for example, are not functionally literate. That's a clear failure of education as such, both in teaching (that people can spend 12+ years in school and not be able to comfortably read) and assessment (that people are allowed to keep moving up without being able to comfortably read). Just fixing that would do a lot, since someone who's substantially literate has much better access to quality information and discussions.

Best argument I've heard so far! The no child left behind system does an incredible disservice to the education level of the average American, you're right about that. However, if we changed that and started holding people back without instilling in people a passion for learning that extends beyond career opportunities you end up with a bloated system in which people keep failing out of their classes. This was part of the reason we ended up here.

However, if we changed that and started holding people back without instilling in people a passion for learning that extends beyond career opportunities you end up with a bloated system in which people keep failing out of their classes.
Some of them, yes, but other students would benefit from actually having the required skills to move on and benefit. Culture certainly plays a role in lack of passion, but feeling confident is also huge. People might disengage anyway, but they're definitely going to disengage if they're trying to learn about literature when they struggle to parse a paragraph.
That said, assessment is only part of it. I think what's more important is that how many US systems teach reading obviously does not work. You really shouldn't need to be holding meaningful numbers of people back over being illiterate, and illiteracy creates a huge problem for all areas of education. (I'm no education specialist and can't really evaluate the quality of the evidence, but I've seen a number of articles about the damage done by shifting away from "phonics" in recent decades, though I guess the trend is starting to reverse. Apparently a lot of schools currently teach people to try to recognize whole words instead of being able to effectively sound them out and work it out from context, which allegedly makes it much harder to become fully literate.)

I'm with you there. You're 100% right that a different system would benefit some student, but those are the students who are already passionate and engaged with school. The majority of people are pretty checked out and probably wouldn't go if it was optional. That's where the cultural issue lies and that would not be different under a different system.

That's where the cultural issue lies and that would not be different under a different system.
That's a hefty assumption. Why can't the school system affect people's attitudes towards education? Have you never had a teacher who inspired you to engage more with a specific topic (or drove you away)? It can't be the whole solution, but it's absolutely possible that developing better basic literacy would encourage people to engage more with books and education more broadly, and it's wildly implausible that the place where people spend 30-40 hours a week for 12+ years of their life doesn't affect their attitude about it.
I think you're severely underestimating the extent to which something being presented in an interesting and accessible way can drive engagement (and the lack thereof can kill it). And basic literacy is crucial to just about anything being accessible.

Oh yeah I agreed with that. My point was that if you want people to be literate it takes a lot more than teaching them that in school. The literacy rate decline has directly coincided with an aversion to reading as a passtime outside of school. That passion will not return unless we can justify it for its own sake.

That passion will not return unless we can justify it for its own sake.
Sure, but can't school help with that? Exposing students to interesting literature and the skills to appreciate it (preferably while not aggressively murdering their enjoyment of it through awful book-report assignments) should encourage at least some of them to value it for its own sake.
I actually picked up my interest in reading (and I'm a serious reader; 17 books so far in 2026) first in school, though I honed it at home. I thought the pictures in a Zoobooks magazine were so interesting I eventually decided I ought to find out what the descriptions said.

Yeah you're right it would help. But I bet we would still be lagging behind education wise unless we actually address our bare minimum attitude when it comes to learning. Honestly though, I can see how this is very subjective, especially with how different everyone's experience in school was.

CMV: Society should push back against "not being able to take being asked out as a question" just as much as "not being able to take no for an answer". Prompted by this post. A man met a woman at a coffee shop (they're both regulars). They had a few conversations and then the man asked out the woman. The woman rejected him because she already had a boyfriend. The man was understanding and stopped asking her.
The man then told a coworker, and the coworker told him that what he did was creepy. The comment were overwhelmingly NTA, and people were even saying that they don't like people who think/act like the coworker.
I think there needs to be a lot more pushback against people like the coworker. The man did everything right : asked her out at an appropriate place (a coffee shop), got to know her (so they weren't strangers), and politely backed off when she said she already had a boyfriend. Yet he was still labelled a creep. Right now, a lot of men are afraid to ask out anyone at all, due to fear of being labelled a creep or weirdo. This is not reasonable.
I think people need to make a very clear statement about this: If a man asks out a woman in a place intended for socializing, gets to know her, and immediately stops pursuing her if she rejects him once, then it's not creepy, not sexual harassment, and the man does not deserve any negative labels such as "creep" or "weirdo". It doesn't matter how ugly, unattractive or socially awkward he is. He is not a creep. I think most of the people saying "NTA" agree with that statement.
But I don't think it's enough to just say that. We need to further and call out the people labelling those men as creeps (such as the coworker in the other thread). If someone says things like "I was a club/event and some weirdo asked me out, I just want to do the activity in peace, why can't men leave me alone", I think we should tell them "No, the weirdo here is you, not him. He asked you out and then dropped it as soon as you rejected him. He didn't do anything wrong. You're the weirdo for labelling him a weirdo when he did what he everything he was supposed to do correctly". (of course, the caveat here is that the man must have actually done everything correctly. if he kept asking despite being rejected, then he actually is a creep and deserves to be called a creep).
I think that it's necessary to call out people labelling completely normal, kind, good men who respect women as creeps. Otherwise the result is that men are afraid to approach women and choose not to (and that includes the cute guy that you are always hoping would ask you out some day). There is already a lot of men who just never ask out any woman because they're afraid of being labelled a creep or sexual harasser. And then single women who are looking for a boyfriend are wondering why nobody asks them out anymore.

While in a perfect world you'd be right, the truth is that right now for every "normal" interaction of this kind a typical woman has where a man asks her out, she says no, and he's ok with it and acts normal about it, there are multiple interactions where women feel unsafe in such situations because the men do indeed act creepy about it and don't take rejections well. So while this particular guy did everything right and was labelled a creep by his coworker undeservedly, what the coworker said did have some truth in it - women are often asked out in random places in ways that make them uncomfortable. And that should be called out. It's too bad some normal people are caught in the backlash, but until women can feel safe rejecting such advances, men should be aware that they can inadvertently make a woman very uncomfortable even if they are chill and have the best intentions. The woman doesn't know whether he's "one of the good ones".
What should men do then, never ask out anyone, at all? Ever? Because the woman can't read the man's mind and is afraid of him?

No, they should model good behaviour so that women can learn to feel safe and not feel the need to try and read their mind, but also be aware that if a woman does get defensive or someone else perceives their advance as creepy it's not about them personally, but about a long history of men acting badly. This guy made a post and got reassured that he acted ok and was NTA. But it's good that he double-checked. And his coworker overreacted in this case, but sometimes it's better to call out five creeps and one non-creep accidentally than not call out the creeps so as not to accidentally insult the one who is good. Change takes time, especially systemic change. Women were taught over generations to be scared of rejecting men. Unlearning this will take effort on both sides. Yes, women shouldn't assume that every guy is a creep and might hurt them. But if a man does something risky and someone reacts poorly, this is also not a reason to turn on all the women and allies.
This situation shows things going into the right direction - the man did things well, the woman reacted well, the coworker didn't, the internet took the side of the man not his coworker. Piling onto the coworker now and trying to correct it into the other direction undoes the progress. He overshot with his judgement, but his heart was clearly in the right place. It happens. He will learn and so will the women, as long as more good men keep acting well even if they aren't instantly praised for doing the right thing.

Before we continue this discussion, I want to ask : do you agree with the coworker and think that the man in the other thread was being creepy?
women can learn to feel safe and not feel the need to try and read their mind
What does that look like? What should men do to make women feel safer? Would saying "I understand" and backing off work? You're not allowed to answer "never ask them out".

do you agree with the coworker and think that the man in the other thread was being creepy?
No, I don't. If the story happened exactly as the post describes and the coworker didn't have any additional information that OOP failed to include in his post, the coworker clearly overreacted and shouldn't have called OP a creep.
What I disagree with in your post is that we should call out people like the coworker with the same energy we call out men who approach women in an actually creepy manner. We can reassure OOP that his coworker was in the wrong in this particular case and OOP can continue approaching women the way he does. And encourage other men to do the same. And leave the coworker alone in his misdirected good intentions.
Yes, it's unpleasant to be labeled a creep by one coworker when you didn't actually do anything creepy and needing to set the record straight. But it's much worse to be approached by an actual creep and not being believed, because labeling people as creeps is suddenly as bad as being a creep. These things don't deserve the same criticism. That's all I'm arguing.
What does that look like? What should men do to make women feel safer? Would saying "I understand" and backing off work?
Yes exactly. And if the woman still feels unsafe because she was traumatized by some other dude before not acting this way, being understanding about it and not whining that "oh my god, you can't ask anybody out, because you'll be labeled a creep even if you're good". This might happen. It sucks. It will happen less if there are fewer creeps being creepy. Saying that women and allies are too hysterical will just encourage the creeps unfortunately thus undermining the good work of not being one.

I'll award a !delta for this:
What I disagree with in your post is that we should call out people like the coworker with the same energy we call out men who approach women in an actually creepy manner ... But it's much worse to be approached by an actual creep and not being believed, because labeling people as creeps is suddenly as bad as being a creep. These things don't deserve the same criticism.
I agree that actually being a creep is worse than falsely being labelled a creep by a coworker.
It seems like you agree with me that the man in the original thread wasn't a creep, and what he did was right.

I've seen people shoot their shot in line at the grocery with no creep factor but this is when there is obvious chemistry.
I think an interesting way to rethink this is if the scenario is they are both men at a coffee shop and nothing in their conversations suggested any romantic possibility or impossibility or inviting assumptions as to sexual orientation for either.
What would you think in this situation? If one guy propositions the other is it a creep move?

If one guy propositions the other is it a creep move?
Depends on how he handles rejection. If he takes it nicely, not creepy. If he keeps asking/pressuring, creepy.
If the nice way he handles rejection is to apologize if it came off creepy (e.g. "sorry I realize that was a little out of the blue", would you be against that way of handling it?

"sorry I realize that was a little out of the blue" is fine. I wouldn't be against it.
It's about consent. A man labelling himself creepy is different from another person (such as a coworker) labelling him creepy. In one situation he consented to getting that label. In the other, he didn't consent to being labelled creepy.
Am I right in thinking you would also allow the recipient of his affections to feel this is a creepy transaction and to say so?

Feel it's creepy: yes. Say the action is creepy: maybe. Say the person is a creep : no. There are a myriad of ways to nicely tell someone that you don't like what they're doing, and calling someone a creep is not one of them.

If he uses the same language as in the other case: "I must tell you that's a little out of the blue" I'm guessing that is civil enough, but why isn't that essentially just a nice way of saying he's being a creep?
The euphemism people often use is "being forward" but it basically means creep, right?

Not sure why "being forward" is a euphemism for creep.
Anyways, he's allowed to call himself a creep, because he consented to it. The OOP didn't consent to being labelled a creep by the coworker. Consent is important.

CMV: Referendums are dumb and direct democracy will end our civilisation if it ever becomes a thing Brexit proves it. CMV: There's nothing good about breeding dogs and cats. TW*, mentions of death*
Hello (I am new here),
I have an opinion that is no doubt controversial among the pet owners community and haven't found any reason to change my opinion yet, so I wanted to have the opportunity to hear some arguments against it.
I fully support the well-known statement "Adopt don't shop" and aim to side with the rescue community when it comes to pet adoption. I've rescued strays and fostered shelter pets that were at risk of euthanasia, volunteered countless times and have my own shelter-adopted pup. I plan on building some kennels in my backyard so I can foster even more pets.
I think it is sad that so many shelters either have to turn down pets in need or have to euthanize the ones they currently have, all due to the lack of adopters. I've seen it first hand and have met healthy shelter dogs and cats when volunteering that were euthanized the next day because of capacity. They could've been somebody's beloved best friend, but their lives were ended instead. It's heartbreaking.
Statistically, over 3 million dogs and cats are sold through breeders every year in the US alone. About 600,000 pets were euthanized for space in US shelters in 2024.
Here's the thing - that means despite popular belief, there are enough adopters to save all the pets that would be euthanized and more beyond that. But instead, people are buying pets from breeders and retail pet shops. If just 20% of the buyers instead chose to adopt, no pet would need to die for capacity at a shelter. And if more than that chose to adopt? They could clean out the shelters, lightening the load of pets taken in by shelters and rescues and giving so many shelter pets the chance to thrive.
Yet, breeders continue to breed pets when there's already so many that need to be adopted in the first place and promote their pets over shelter pets. People buy these pets instead of rescuing a pet in need. No, buyers did not kill a pet by adopting a bred pet despite what some people say, it's not like they bought a bred pet and then went and killed a shelter pet. What they did do is miss out on the easy, available opportunity to save a life, and also funded and supported an operation (dog/cat breeding) that contributes to more people choosing to not save a life. It all ends in death for innocent animals.
**
I see puppy mills and "reputable" breeders as the same category, contributing to the same results. Picture it this way (a little dark but I'll keep it not too graphic), there are two serial k!llers! One k!ller taunts and starves their victims before violently unaliving them. The second one is nice to their victims, gives them food and water and treats them politely before unaliving them.
Okay. That was dark for sure, but do you see the pattern? Both instances end in the same result of death. It doesn't matter how it was performed, it still ends in something inhumane and unethical.
This is how I see puppy mills and reputable breeders. They do their operations much differently but it all ends in the same result - dead shelter animals. On a side note, this is actually a lot like how our society works - something completely evil can be seen as "okay" if it begins ethically. And that's just wrong!
Anyway, both mills and breeders contribute to less pets being adopted and more pets being euthanized. If all puppy mills and breeders quit their operations for good, people would seek out shelter pets and thus save lives. But because mills and breeders still breed pets, that happy ending isn't in sight for now.
Many also blame irresponsible pet owners for the number of strays. The owned pets are not fixed and run away and mate with strays, causing a larger population of strays that end up in shelters. Who sold the pets to the irresponsible owners in the first place though? Couldn't be rescues or shelters, almost all of them fix their pets prior! Oh right, it's mills and breeders since they usually don't fix their sold pets!
Not to mention all the deadly dog attacks that happen because of the number of strays that came from breeders...
This post's topics aren't well organized (sorry) but another thing I wanted to point out is more than 1/4 of all shelter pets are purebreds/designers, which adopters seek. That's millions of pets, but people still go to breeders thanks to their promotion and whatnot.
And one more random piece of evidence, most purebred animals have issues thanks to inbreeding. French Bulldogs can't breathe half the time, German Shepherds have hip issues, etcetera. Mixed breeds are healthier for this reason. And, *besides people with allergies* why do we need certain breeds nowadays? Most people don't use dogs for herding and hunting, they're companion animals now. Dogs are all the same really, so why do we need certain breeds? There's wonderful mutts of all sizes and ages. And even if everyone adopted shelter pets, your favorite breeds aren't going to be snapped out of existence or something.
And last random fact, dogs/cats are NOT going to disappear if breeders stopped. Stray populations will still exist (just in much smaller numbers) and there will still be pets available, but without breeders there wouldn't be a crazy amount of strays/euthanasia.
**
If everybody adopted a shelter pet, they'd all be saved and loved. Stray populations would slowly decline with more people adopting desexed pets. Dog attacks would lessen and genetic purebred issues would lessen, since breeders wouldn't be inbreeding animals. It would lead to a more humane world with less pets dying and more pets getting the chance to live a happy life with an owner. And I think breeding pets for profit is really abusing the fact that pets like us. Most animals run upon the sight of a human, but pets don't. They love and trust us, and breeding them like products is really disrespectful.
**
I will never buy a pet from a breeder no doubt, but from this all said, I think breeding dogs and cats for selling (guide dogs/police dogs don't concern me btw) is unethical and plain bad and should be stopped. No one should buy a bred pet and everyone should adopt.
Can you CMV?
~Kira
EDIT: I was more so referring to not breeding for companion pets, I think breeding can be valid if it is for a very specific purpose (e.g. police dogs, guide dogs, herding, etc.).
EDIT: I keep saying this to everybody so I'll just write it here. For people NOT looking to experiment with a shelter pet's temperament, they can always adopt a puppy or kitten. Train them from day 1. No bite history or past trauma.
Also breed specific rescues exist for people seeking certain breeds.
I think some people need specialised dogs eg guide dogs and you can’t just adopt those ?
I think you can adopt and train dogs but for guide dogs I think breeding is valid since its a specific purpose, idk.
Delta plz
alr i haven't had my mind completely changed tho, i still don't believe in breeding for companions but its valid for specific purposes, Δ

Another thing to consider, selective breeding can be used to fix genetic deformities we’ve created in dogs via years of only breeding for aesthetics. In the long term this might be the only viable way to prevent dogs being born that may look cute, but suffer so much when they are alive
Selective breeding mistakes can fix themselves without more selective breeding tho, if street dogs only bred there would only be mutts free of major genetic issues.
Not really, I think some dogs contain dominant genes that will be passed on via random natural breeding with steet dogs
That is true and I know but the worst traits will be filtered out over time through more natural breeding.

I guess if you had a dog with genes that were harmful, either you neuter it which is cruel because it’s denying it the opportunity to engage in species typical behaviour OR you allow it to breed via having its genetic material selected via ways that ensure its harmful traits are not passed on
Neutering is the opposite of cruel. Dogs don't need to mate to exist, and mating actually increases the risk of cancers. And not neutering ends in oops litters.

CMV: All charities should be boycotted To start this post, I do not care about the US, I am British, I will be talking about Britain. This may apply to some European countries too.
Edit: This is also assuming perfectly benevolent charities, I'm not going into the corruption and failure to do their job that happens for some charities. Even if every charity was perfect my point still applies.
My main arguments are,
- Charities only exist because of the under provision of the state
- Charities are fundamentally undemocratic
- So all morally essential goods should be provided through democratic taxation.
and here's why.
I remember the day - many years ago - that I found out that air ambulances are fully funded by charity. I was dumbfounded, air ambulances are not luxuries but are central parts of healthcare infrastructure. We have an NHS that will pay for expensive cancer treatments, and all the ambulance trips in the world, but not the money for helicopters? We have to hope that people continue personally giving money to these charities to continue operating these vital bits of healthcare?
Air ambulances are a stark example of how charities fill the gaps of where the government fails to provide essential services, without giving the government the opportunity to actually do it's job. Another similar example is the RNLI, a charity that acts as a coast guard in the UK. But I argue every charity is so.
Everyone remembers Red Nose day, an initiative by a charity Comic Relief who spends a lot of their money helping children who are in poverty in the UK. Having children in poverty in the 5th richest country in the world is a horrific stain on our nation. Instead of the government tackling it by decreasing the inequality within society, we have charities plug the gap by feeding hungry children. Comic relief is one of many UK poverty charities, be it local food banks, "warm spaces" for pensioners unable to afford heating, Children In Need and so on. These lift the political pressure on politicians to actually fix the issues that cause these charities to exist.
There are the medical research charities, such as Cancer Research UK or British Heart Foundation, who fund scientists to find cures to diseases killing many young people. It's the NHS's responsibility to both provide care, and also improve the care it gives. The government should be funding medical research to make it's own population healthier.
The big one is foreign aid charities, and seemingly harder to justify, the Concert for Bangladesh being the first, but you have countless others like Oxfam, UNICEF and so on. Foreign aid returns dividends for governments, firstly it spreads its soft power all over the world. When an African country is wondering which country should build it's new infrastructure, it will go for the country that helped them get into the position of having that infrastructure. Or when a bright child, who remembers growing up eating food with "UK" stamped on the boxes, wonders where he will work, he will be thinking there. China understands this well and is spreading its soft power everywhere.
It also stabilises the world, dire poverty or famine pushes people towards extremist groups, international terrorist organisations that attack the west can be disarmed by feeding the people for whome they extreamise. There's also the reduction in refugees who come flooding to Europe costing billions. Or the improved health, reducing the chances of global pandemics.
You have wildlife and education charities too, but for brevity I will let you imagine how I would argue they're socially essential. And so should not rely on philanthropy.
So I've blabbered on about how charities just do the work that the government should be doing, but what's the issue, why should there be a boycott?
Charities are undemocratic, the people with the most money have the greatest control of how the funds are distributed. This means that, if the general population wants to deal with child poverty, but the very richest were particularly moved by an advert about pandas, and they don't worry about poverty, then the money goes to saving Pandas over starving children.
The money should be collected, and then everyone should have an equal say in how the money should be distributed, what causes are the most important, ie, the government does the job that charities currently do. And I've shown the overlap is unity.
Bekkers & Wiepking (2011) — “A Literature Review of Donor Behaviour” shows how the amount of money that individuals spend on donations is fixed, however they just allocate it differently. So if one charity spends lots of money on advertising and awareness and rakes in a tonne of money for a good cause, they are unwittingly stealing money from other charities.
This creates a competitive market for charities, who have to fight for the attention and thus money from the population. They use heartbreaking footage of Polar Bears stuck on a floating iceberg, or a starving Sudanese child, designed to illicit an emotional response, rather than a rational decision to which would cause the most good, or be the most socially essential. Instead we can have governmental committees on the distribution of funds, spending the money for the most good, not the best feelings. There would also be a lot more money going to the causes, as there wouldn't be the money spent on advertising and awareness.
The only way to get to a world where governments do their jobs and we don't have or need charities is to show what happens when there isn't the plaster covering the wound. Only once the plaster is removed can the wounds heal.
It will be really bad for the short term before the government has insurmountable pressure on it as people see the failures, so they step up and start properly taxing people (and maybe stop giving pensioners constant raises) to perform all of their societal imperatives. As ultimately, this system of societal necessities being chosen by the wealthy cannot continue.
So that's why I believe there should be a boycott of charities. Change my view.

Boycotting is not the effective strategy here.
We absolutely agree on the premise, that all socially necessary production and services should be done by the government. However boycotting the people filling the gap is not helpful, it’s preventing some benefit to other working class people for no upside.
Instead like most problems, we should seek to tackle this from the ground up, forcing government to take on that responsibility first and eliminating the need for the charity work. No sense in doing it the other way around and letting people suffer. If the government refuses, well we might just need a new government then.

How can we force the government to take on the responsibility when there is no issue as charity is dealing with it?
Going back to the bandage example, how can you heal the wound if you've got a plaster covering it? You need the blood to flow and clot before you can heal?
And when you say "a new government", do you mean a different political party within the same system, or to change the system? Because this issue is endemic to the current way of government.

I mean a complete change of our system. It’s clear to anyone looking that these are systemic problems that a simple change between parties with the same elite interests isn’t going to solve.
However we are not going to win anyone to our side by arguing against food banks. We might not see them as ultimately productive but reforms like them are important in building consciousness and political will. It’s a lot more progressive for a mass amount of people to be engaging directly in their local communities and starting to learn about their own power to make a change than to continue putting their faith in distant politicians.
It's not that food banks and other charities aren't productive, it's that they're an active hindrance to change.

This is perhaps true in the abstract but not as a policy position.
It’s true that people feeling the effects of the system failing helps move them towards more revolutionary conclusions. However it’s important that the blame is directed at the people responsible, those in power. It’s not worth the potential to turn people against whatever organization or movement you’re a part of by attacking the things that help them get by. At the end of the day charities are fundamentally incapable of making up the difference here and will inevitably fail.
Don’t let impatience decide your strategy, better a successful movement in five or ten years than a bungled sectarian mess in two.

You know what, that deserves a !delta , the risk of the hatred going elsewhere is large. I would be interested in what a longer term strategy would be you suggest? I could think of lots of messaging and convincing of the ideas of class consciousness.

There are three main pillars of this. For transparency, I’m a Marxist, what I’ll be describing below is the general shape of how we operate.
The first is building a vanguard. There are new movements every year and people who become disillusioned when each fails. The goal this early on is to find these people who will be able to act as leaders in the future. Without some amount of people learning the lessons of past revolutions ahead of time, we’re bound to repeat their mistakes. These are the people that in the heat of things can lead a factory occupation, a protest march, or resistance against police violence. Without them you get tragedies like the failure of the German revolution or the crushing of the UK’s miner strikes.
The second is policy and consistency. Legitimacy isn’t built overnight, we have to consistently maintain positive positions and communicate them, both to build up active recognition and to have a long history to point back at when things heat up. This is the point that led me to opposing your idea, you don’t want people going through the history of your positions and figuring out you’re the one who at least directly if not systematically made them go hungry.
The third is supporting events and movements that build consciousness, things like strikes. A great recent example is the resistance to ICE in the US, it’s done a lot of good for class consciousness there. This is the most flexible, class consciousness builds eventually under capitalism but we don’t want it to take too long and allow people to continue suffering.
There’s more obviously but I’d say these are the main pillars. If this is the kind of thing you’re interested in, feel free to DM me, I’m in an organization with a very strong British section. Otherwise thank you for the lively discussion.

CMV: All charities should be boycotted To start this post, I do not care about the US, I am British, I will be talking about Britain. This may apply to some European countries too.
Edit: This is also assuming perfectly benevolent charities, I'm not going into the corruption and failure to do their job that happens for some charities. Even if every charity was perfect my point still applies.
My main arguments are,
- Charities only exist because of the under provision of the state
- Charities are fundamentally undemocratic
- So all morally essential goods should be provided through democratic taxation.
and here's why.
I remember the day - many years ago - that I found out that air ambulances are fully funded by charity. I was dumbfounded, air ambulances are not luxuries but are central parts of healthcare infrastructure. We have an NHS that will pay for expensive cancer treatments, and all the ambulance trips in the world, but not the money for helicopters? We have to hope that people continue personally giving money to these charities to continue operating these vital bits of healthcare?
Air ambulances are a stark example of how charities fill the gaps of where the government fails to provide essential services, without giving the government the opportunity to actually do it's job. Another similar example is the RNLI, a charity that acts as a coast guard in the UK. But I argue every charity is so.
Everyone remembers Red Nose day, an initiative by a charity Comic Relief who spends a lot of their money helping children who are in poverty in the UK. Having children in poverty in the 5th richest country in the world is a horrific stain on our nation. Instead of the government tackling it by decreasing the inequality within society, we have charities plug the gap by feeding hungry children. Comic relief is one of many UK poverty charities, be it local food banks, "warm spaces" for pensioners unable to afford heating, Children In Need and so on. These lift the political pressure on politicians to actually fix the issues that cause these charities to exist.
There are the medical research charities, such as Cancer Research UK or British Heart Foundation, who fund scientists to find cures to diseases killing many young people. It's the NHS's responsibility to both provide care, and also improve the care it gives. The government should be funding medical research to make it's own population healthier.
The big one is foreign aid charities, and seemingly harder to justify, the Concert for Bangladesh being the first, but you have countless others like Oxfam, UNICEF and so on. Foreign aid returns dividends for governments, firstly it spreads its soft power all over the world. When an African country is wondering which country should build it's new infrastructure, it will go for the country that helped them get into the position of having that infrastructure. Or when a bright child, who remembers growing up eating food with "UK" stamped on the boxes, wonders where he will work, he will be thinking there. China understands this well and is spreading its soft power everywhere.
It also stabilises the world, dire poverty or famine pushes people towards extremist groups, international terrorist organisations that attack the west can be disarmed by feeding the people for whome they extreamise. There's also the reduction in refugees who come flooding to Europe costing billions. Or the improved health, reducing the chances of global pandemics.
You have wildlife and education charities too, but for brevity I will let you imagine how I would argue they're socially essential. And so should not rely on philanthropy.
So I've blabbered on about how charities just do the work that the government should be doing, but what's the issue, why should there be a boycott?
Charities are undemocratic, the people with the most money have the greatest control of how the funds are distributed. This means that, if the general population wants to deal with child poverty, but the very richest were particularly moved by an advert about pandas, and they don't worry about poverty, then the money goes to saving Pandas over starving children.
The money should be collected, and then everyone should have an equal say in how the money should be distributed, what causes are the most important, ie, the government does the job that charities currently do. And I've shown the overlap is unity.
Bekkers & Wiepking (2011) — “A Literature Review of Donor Behaviour” shows how the amount of money that individuals spend on donations is fixed, however they just allocate it differently. So if one charity spends lots of money on advertising and awareness and rakes in a tonne of money for a good cause, they are unwittingly stealing money from other charities.
This creates a competitive market for charities, who have to fight for the attention and thus money from the population. They use heartbreaking footage of Polar Bears stuck on a floating iceberg, or a starving Sudanese child, designed to illicit an emotional response, rather than a rational decision to which would cause the most good, or be the most socially essential. Instead we can have governmental committees on the distribution of funds, spending the money for the most good, not the best feelings. There would also be a lot more money going to the causes, as there wouldn't be the money spent on advertising and awareness.
The only way to get to a world where governments do their jobs and we don't have or need charities is to show what happens when there isn't the plaster covering the wound. Only once the plaster is removed can the wounds heal.
It will be really bad for the short term before the government has insurmountable pressure on it as people see the failures, so they step up and start properly taxing people (and maybe stop giving pensioners constant raises) to perform all of their societal imperatives. As ultimately, this system of societal necessities being chosen by the wealthy cannot continue.
So that's why I believe there should be a boycott of charities. Change my view.

Charities are definitely worse than bigger democratic systems for the reasons you suggest. However, you're running off of the pretty big assumption that a boycott of charitable giving would force the government to act in a particular way. You say it will be really bad in the short term before the pressure is insurmountable, but what if it's instead bad in the short term and then it continues to be bad in the long term? If my observation of politics has taught me anything, it's that the government is excellent at ignoring pressure I would otherwise imagine to be insurmountable.
If you're wrong in this way, and I think there's an excellent chance you are, then you're just causing tons of death in exchange for nothing. What you are describing, ultimately, is accelerationism. Making things get bad under the assumption that it will cause them to get better. But things just get worse a lot of the time. People have experienced tons of hardship without it yielding luxury space communism. At the end of the day, I think we're better off trying to do good things. Even if you don't succeed entirely at such an effort, the output is usually pretty decent at least.

Marxists argue that bourgeois charity keeps capitalism propped up, keeps the workers just happy enough to be contempt and not achieve class conscientiousness.
I don't go as far as saying it would cause a full revolution against capitalism, but it would seriously improve the collective wellbeing of society.
We have seen the bourgeoisie employ other tactics to avoid class consciousness, such as overly blaming people in the outgroup as the source of problems rather than themselves, and I do fear that those tactics could hinder the people's response to the boycott.
I am definitely an acceleratist economically, but I'm not asking for the world. I'm asking for air ambulances to be funded by the government, to increase foreign aid budgets, or to remove the triple lock. I'm not saying it would cause the revolution, it will just get us closer, and along the way we will have a better society.

Wealth inequality has been increasing for decades, as has the gap between wages and productivity. We're watching in real time as reactionary billionaires buy up the media landscape piece by piece, turning it into a shell of its former self. We saw a bunch of rich assholes defraud the market until it set the whole economy on fire and get away scot-free. None of this has yielded some grand uplifting of revolutionary spirit, an awakening of the communist utopia living in all of our hearts. Hell, most of that happened before Trump got elected for a second time. A lot of people see horror and blame minorities. I see no precedent for any of this working. What I do see precedent for is bad things happening and people dying from it.

None of this has yielded some grand uplifting of revolutionary spirit
No, because they are propping the proles up and material conditions haven't collapsed enough.
The French revolution is a great precedent of what can happen. Sometimes it takes starving populations to enact change.

And you've just decided that people boycotting charity would exceed all these horrors I've described that already exist? Cause I'm skeptical. I do not think you are engaging sufficiently with what I see as a highly plausible outcome, which is that a lot of people die and nothing good is produced.
The revolutionary drive only ends when people's material conditions improve, and that's the goal is it not?

Is it? My goal is for people not to die. For their lives to be good and for them to be treated with human decency. If the grand communist vision is a good one, then it is because it seems like a good way to achieve those ends. Because wealth inequality makes people have worse lives.
This is why I, for example, support the idea of charity moving to the public sector. Because leaving the decision to a handful of assholes who have very particular interests is a great way to make bad things happen. Because handing them the reins of society does not respect human beings. But what you suggest, leaving the disadvantaged to die on the street, is not serving this end. It's doing the exact opposite. It's placing the bet that hurting people now will hurt them less later. And, as cruel as placing such a bet seems, it's crueler when you consider the high chance it will not work.
I also do really have to point out, because I haven't so far, this whole thing is incredibly impractical and kinda silly. What you are talking about is having left leaning people exclusively stop donating. Right wingers aren't going to be interested in creating communism. The result, then, is that the cool lefty types come off as hypocritical shitheads who will happily let children die on the street if it means scoring political points, while the Christian nationalists and billionaires come off as the saviors of humanity who are continuing to help people live their lives. Yeah, lots of people are gonna support the cause after that.

This is why I, for example, support the idea of charity moving to the public sector
Wonderful, glad we can agree
I also do really have to point out, because I haven't so far, this whole thing is incredibly impractical and kinda silly.
Oh yeah, the post is "there should be", rather than "there will be".
I just feel your not accelerationist enough.

I already agreed. It was the literal first thing I did. And yeah, I'm not accelerationist because accelerationism has no proven track record (French revolutionaries were not trying to make things worse at any point), has an incredibly obvious downside of doing the literal exact opposite of what you're trying to do, and has the equally obvious risk of just making things worse without ever creating your promised utopia.
Bluntly, things go to shit sometimes. Like, they just go to shit, and they stay shit for awhile, and the good times this promises are literally just returning to the pre-shit status quo. Moreover, there are a lot of people who like things being shit. Accelerationism is a political theory where you team up with a bunch of fascists and losers under the assumption that, while they think awful nonsense leads to awful nonsense (which they like), you think awful nonsense will lead to a perfect world. I write it out explicitly like that and it just seems deeply absurd. Making things be shit usually just makes things shit.

CMV: The USA should have completely open borders. (EDIT: This is about the political concept of open borders, NOT a literal open border. A plane from North Korea can’t just land at a US airport without documentation. You can still track people coming in or out and punish those who evade such tracking.)
Economics:
Quantifiably and statistically, immigrants are good for the country. Illegal immigrants are still good for the country- in many ways, exploitatively better than legal immigrants. They pay sales taxes, work for low wages, produce goods, consume, and most are unable to take advantage of welfare. Immigration in every developed country brings a higher birth rate, which is necessary for a healthy economy.
On the individual personal level, if an individual can’t compete with another individual- the capitalist’s answer is… “tough luck. Get gud.”
A business or a national economy that excludes workers for being more competitive is a business or economy that will itself be less competitive.
Crime:
Immigrants generally commit less crime and have more crimes committed against them because they are afraid of deportation. Without that fear, they might be a little more brazen but will also cooperate with police more readily.
Illegal immigrants are unwittingly a large part of many financial or extortionate crimes. They don’t report wrongdoing because they fear reprisal or deportation. Many employers who use illegal immigrants as labor prefer it this way.
Cultural/Social:
America is the melting pot. There is no danger of ‘white erasure’ and even if there was, you get ‘American Culture’ as an evolution of whatever came before. From Jazz to The Last Airbender, Yoga to Saint Patrick’s Day. The worldwide cultural influences have pretty much only been positive. Name a ‘bad habit’ we picked up from immigrants. The only reason to feel anxiety about demographics is if you put a lot of emphasis on the skin color of your descendants… which is, y’know, cringe.
Historical:
It’s usually been groups like the KKK on the side of anti-immigration. Nothing more really to be said. I’m not arguing ‘guilt by association’ but more like ‘There is a reason this position appeals so much to those kinds of people’
Immigration in the 19th century has quantifiably and unquestionably been better for the country. Many of the problems associated with immigrants are really just other problems scapegoated onto them. Poverty. Organized crime. Y’know, stuff that usually has a white guy behind it.
Political/Voting:
The fear is that immigrants will be easily persuaded to vote for a candidate that promises to represent them better than the other guy. …okay? Isn’t that democracy? A guy promising to do a good job for your interests? Not like Americans aren’t easily duped too.
Like the demographic/historical reasons, there really is no justification for this beyond those in power wanting to keep power- at the expense of American values of egalitarianism.
Conclusion:
Just let anyone in. I don’t care. When you step foot in this country, you enjoy all the legal rights and responsibilities of a citizen. Just like anyone else, you have to register residency to vote, can’t commit crimes, pay taxes you are responsible for, collect benefits you are eligible for, etc. Just no distinction.
Free soil because all men are created equal, whether they were born right here or off over there.

The problem is that illegal immigration causes social tension, and you can’t just ignore that, because that is ultimately bad for the country. A united country is a stronger country. In Australia, we have virtually zero illegal immigration, we are an island with strong border controls. This means we control everyone who enters the country. Because we have such a degree of control, and we can select people who are going to be less of a problem, as well as more educated/skilled people, and our population can happily sustain a far higher number of foreign born immigrants than anywhere else in the world without social tension. Something like 30% of our population is foreign born. I Would argue that like Australia, if the US got control of its own border, American society could allow and sustain a higher immigration number than now, with less social tension. Because the people that are allowed in are selected to be better suited to American life and values. Personally I really don’t understand why American leftists subscribe to the open borders theory, it’s quite illogical to run a country like that.

Again, the history…

If this is your goal, then you would be actively emboldening the white supremacists in your country, because they would sound technically right.
“The democrats are flooding the country with illegal immigrants to replace white people” is partly true if you are actually flooding the country with illegal immigrant’s to combat white supremacy. So more people will agree with their message and the movement will grow
In Australia, we also have Neo-Nazis, but they are very small and not taken seriously because all of the immigrants are here legitimately. So their message holds a lot less weight. Again I would argue that by controlling your border, you would legitimise immigrants and then you would have less tensions overall, including from the white suprematist camp.

Like is this what you want for your country?
This is why I’m saying that your plan is based on ideology, not practicality. If you actually wanted to reduce white supremacist influence you need to make them sound wrong, and you do that by legitimising immigration by controlling the border.
Like this actually worked in my country. it sounds counterintuitive but it works. We have strong borders, and our foreign born population is around 30%. In America it’s only 15%. So literally double.

CMV: Decoupling benefits from the employer would be a fair bargain for employees Imagine this alternative labor regime, all employment has all benefits made mobile that goes with them wherever they work, and is not dependent on the employer. Health insurance, PTO, and pay would be tied to the individual wherever they worked. Every employer would hire employees and then contribute to the employees' accounts for their benefits and the employees would be able to bank or spend their benefits as they see fit. The funds that go towards PTO and health care benefits could be in interests bearing accounts or in funds that collectively invest in T-bills and municipal bonds. The employee, not the employer, would be competed for by health insurance companies (which I would like to see states and municipal governments offer coverage like public option competing with the private sector, similar to how Germany does it) creating downward pressure on costs for health insurance which is not present when the employer chooses insurance for a captured market of their employees.
These mobile benefits would grant independence to the employees and be regulated to be funded by the employers at a base level on the hourly basis, and so there wouldn't be any savings for employers to keep their employees as part-time since they are obligated to provide contributions to the employees no matter how many hours they worked. For example, health insurance needs to be 8% of the agreed wages and PTO needs to be 12% or whatever, so if the payrate is $15 an hour health benefits would be $1.20/hr and PTO would be $1.80 above the $15/hr wage. This would also liberate employees who no longer would fear leaving an employer because of health insurance.
I would also provide universal SNAP benefits of $50 a month, those who qualify for more would get more, but everyone would be able to elect to add more of their pre-tax dollars to their SNAP account thereby saving themselves for taxes that they currently pay on the income they spend on groceries. This would implemented in a 2nd New Deal that would be solving the problem of widespread issue of Americans working multiple jobs rather than the social safety net built for a single breadwinner who works for only one or two employers during their career.
Basically what I'm looking for to change my mind is what would be the biggest pitfalls of something like this if implemented.
In practice this would result in nobody having health insurance.

Or that insurance becomes a competitive consumer market, since every potential consumer could switch just like one can switch their cell phone provider and no consumer would be stuck in the plan their employer chose for them.

The problem is that for most employer-supplied plans, the employer usually pay a substantial amount of the monthly premium. This would end and the employee wouldn't get that money (heck people are barely paid at all these days).
Without that money, most wouldn't afford the premiums. Even on the ACA marketplace a typical high-deductible family plan is easily $1500 or more a month. Most people can't afford to spend the $30k or more in premiums and deductibles before their insurer steps in and denies them care.

Yeah this would overturn the apple cart for the whole industry. What do you surmise would be the result if every single customer could choose their own plan instead of insurance companies needing to convince a couple of executives for each employer, do you think that the insurance companies will be able to keep those premiums up so high if there is no captured market for their services? I suspect that competing insurance organizations would seek out lower premiums and out of pocket expenses to get customers.
My CMV remains: Is this fair for the employees?

We see a little of this right now with the ACA subsidies not being renewed. Prices did not come down, and a lot of people who depended on those subsidies are now uninsured.
The same will play out if employer-provided health insurance is scrapped.
To be fair, I want every health-insurance law passed since 1970 (and maybe even more) repealed. I'd like to see us go back to the days of non-profit health insurance and non-profit healthcare. It would be cheaper and better for all.

If there was a single market where all health insurance customers were up for grabs to all of the insurance companies competing with each other to get the most of the market as possible. No more capture audience of employees who can't exert their own customer choice, no more relying on the small sliver of decision makers of employers that can be persuaded to impose decisions on the many employees. Decentralized decisions are for the benefit of the employees, allowing them to make their own decisions.
Prior to 1970, this was much easier to do.

Not sure how to get the tube of toothpaste back in the tube and going back to the time of health care without the motive of profit maximization. Even this proposal which keeps the profit motive around would be killed by the industry simply for increasing consumer choice and having real competitive market.

I think change is inevitable. The private health insurance industry, to a degree unmatched by probably any other industry, alienates and angers its customers every single day. The number of people saying "there must be a better way" grows every single day.
The anger is in the end user of the service, not with the decision makers of the employers so I don't see how systemic change comes about.

CMV: All criminals/wrongdoers deserve a path to redemption without cruelty regardless of crime committed So, I've held onto this CMV for a while because I feel somewhat strongly about it but I also recognize that it is philosophically complicated. On that note, I am leaning more towards the philosophy/morality of this view not the logistics (ie: how do we pay for people's food) although you're welcome to bring that up if you want.
This CMV is discussing any criminal from a litterer to a serial killer. I want to strongly stress that this CMV by no means condones those or any specific wrongdoings/illegal activities.
Onto the main show:
I believe that all criminals, regardless of their actions, deserve basic human rights and a path to redemption with cruelty (not torture or the death penalty). Here is why:
1. The Baseline of Decency
I don't believe that human rights should be a reward for being a good (or even just not evil) person. I believe that everyone deserves basic human rights. They shouldn't be conditioned on behavior.
2. Neuroplasticity and the ability to change
We know that our brains have the ability to change our entire lives. Every single day we're alive is a day we can change. A day we can become just a little bit better.
I believe that, because of that, humans should be given the opportunity to change. If we were to end someone's life because of a crime they committed, we'll never know what good they may have done if given the opportunity to change.
3. Consequences vs. Cruelty
I'm not arguing that wrongdoers should simply be let off the hook. I believe that they should face consequences. Depending on the crime, this could be anything from losing the right to vote to being incarcerated (although I do have my issue with that but that's for another time).
I just believe they shouldn't face cruelty.
4. Justice & Revenge
This is almost certainly one of the hardest parts of this view. One may ask how is it fair for a murderer to not die or be given the chance at redemption and rehabilitation when their victim is dead.
Well, this is where we get into the philosophical weeds. I am not an expert on philosophy and the philosophy of justice and revenge is a topic so complex it can (and likely has been) it's own CMV...
but to put it simply, I have what I believe is a utilitarian approach (I think).
In the example of murder, the loss of a human life is a total, permanent and horrific loss. but the death of the murder (or social death via solitary confinement or similar punishments) does not subtract from that loss. all it does is increase wasted human potential
5. What is redemption/rehabilitation?
While this would certainly depend on the crime, I see it as less of a fixed destination and more as a journey in a direction.
But basically 3 things
recognition - recognizing the wrong doing and fully accepting responsibility
change - a demonstratable change in character or thought process
restitution - A commitment to being as "pro-social" as possible (within the constraints of whatever consequence they are facing)
I know some may ask what happens if restitution and rehabilitation conflict with each other (ie: a murderer should restitute by being killed) - for that refer back to point 4.
tl;dr - all criminals and wrong doers deserve the path of redemption... even if they choose to never walk it or only take the first step.
edit: I want to remind folks that the point of this post is to discuss the philosophy and morals of this CMV, not the specific logistics (how would we afford it, where would we get the staff, etc) of the hypothetical system
edit 2: guys, while i appreciate the interest, I again remind you, I am discussing the morals/ethics/philosophy. this is a hypothetical system. moving forward, I am either going to ignore or just respond "logistics" to comments that aren't staying on topic

Mercy to the criminal is cruelty to their victims.
It's damaging to the fabric of society for someone to not be adequately punished for their crimes. The victims, especially when they have no say in the matter, end up without closure, and the trust in society erodes. Especially in egregiously violent crimes where there is loss of life or life-altering damage done, recompense can only be suitably fulfilled by balancing the pain of the victims with the same price paid by the perpetrator of that crime.
As an onlooker, you have no way of really judging whether a crime has been suitably paid for, and in the weak justice systems of the modern West, many crimes aren't suitably punished, which has led to an ever increasing dissatisfaction and mistrust of the justice system. This is basically a death by 1000 cuts, and many people are deeply cynical about justice in the west at this point.
As to the "regardless of the crime committed", I'll just say that all of the above is related to crimes with victims. Laws that are administrative laws, such as ordinances and policies are often unjust laws in the first place. Others such as copyright infringements and IP infringements are dealt with as civil matters adequately, and are often punished far too harshly.

Mercy to the criminal is cruelty to their victims.
This is an excellent point. !delta - you have changed my view. I had thought about the victims although I failed to consider that the good done by mercy given to criminals may cause pain outweighing the pain avoided

CMV: Team-based moral identity weakens democracy by eroding pluralism This is a view I've only had for about an hour, so it may be easy to change! :)
I've been thinking about the current polarization within the US and whether the real problem is the disagreement itself or how we assess morality, especially when there is disagreement. For this discussion, the "teams" I'm referring to are US political parties. I’m not arguing against political parties but rather I’m concerned that a lot of people stop using moral reasoning and instead determine morality based on what is acceptable to their team. More specifically, I'm referring to people:
Treating political affiliation as a complete moral identity
Framing opponents as morally deficient rather than differently reasoned
Reducing complex ethical questions to black-and-white judgments, without consideration for moral principles or philosophies
Assuming that if something is socially acceptable within one’s group, it is therefore ethically justified
For example, if someone is against a particular action when it harms their party but is for it when it harms the other party and there is no moral reasoning to explain the difference, this isn't a moral evaluation at all. History shows us that during periods of pplarization, societies sometimes justify ethically questionable actions out of fear or loyalty. I believe this wouldn't happen as much if people maintained their own moral reasoning, even if every one of their conclusions aligns with their team. I'm trying to avoid partisan examples, so some general ones could be: calling one president's pardon of a criminal moral but not pardons from another president; being OK with free speech restrictions only depending on if they help your party, excusing criminal activity from one politician and not another. Not to say there can't be moral reasoning to arrive at different conclusions in similar circumstances, but my point is that most people do not and allowing that to be commonplace weakens the structural conditions that allow democratic societies to function (acceptance of legitimate opposition, commitment to procedural rules, willingness to accept loss, recognition of shared civic membership).
Ethics is a pluralistic field; there are multiple serious moral frameworks, and they often conflict in hard cases. This is something US society overall accepts, as our rules and laws don't follow a single moral philosophy. Rather, discussion and debate about moral reasoning is what caused/s evolution of rules, laws, and social norms. I believe this pluralism is necessary for democracy to be successful, but we put no focus on it as a society and in fact do the opposite by encouraging people to focus on what is socially acceptable. My view is that democracy requires pluralism. Not just diversity of people, but diversity of moral reasoning.
TLDR: Partisanship is necessary, but when moral reasoning is replaced by tribal certainty, pluralism erodes, which weakens democracy.
ETA: im not comparing parties in any way or saying anything about accepting views. Im saying if you call someone/something immoral, you should back up that claim with moral reasoning.

The destruction of our current system of government, including all those involved, is the only way to end what you are speaking about. More cancer cannot cure cancer.
The polity itself is designed to mutually reinforce the outcomes which you are describing. Culture, media, academia all promote loyalty towards a political party. American education system, extremely poor in the areas of critical thinking and reasoning, emphasizes and rewards group think.
The employment market makes for desperate workers who need secure employment to fund things like education, insurance, and retirement. When employees become unemployed they have no capacity to take their time and breathe, they must latch on to the first opportunity. Americans live a very precarious life which is designed to provoke anxiety and credulity. In other words, a poorly educated, loosely attachedAmerican worker will do anything his employer and the state wants him to do.
Americans will bear any indignity (see well documented evidence the government spies on them, influences social media, censors individuals, engages in endless wars which rob from the future, continual debasement of the currency for the benefit of the wealthy and the state at the expense of working people, etc). It does all of these things and worse, and Americans do nothing about it. America has built a perfect cage, constructed by Americans for themselves and thus Americans have no choice but to admire this thing that they have built.

Do you mean people dont outsource moral.identity by nature, but by design. So, not that people abandon moral reasoning, but that our political incentive structures reward moral absolutism. Is that what you mean?

I think they are mutually reinforcing dynamics. The political parties themselves gamify politics, the people, unable to entertain the notion that they are being gamed, align identity with party (much research backs this up by the way), and society itself is structured in such a way that critical perspectives are both inconvenient and costly towards the individual. 
Obviously it is more complex than this simplification but it reduces to a point which requires wholesale change at the political, societal, and economic levels. None of which our current political system is incentivized to do.

Are you maybe replying to someone else?? Because none of this refers to the view I posted at all. I definitely disagree that we should treat immorality as morally neutral. That quite opposes my view.

Your argument is begging the question that, because moral judgments fall upon party lines, therefore people are abandoning moral reasoning. Is that not central to your argument? The view I posted for discussion is that team-based moral identity weakens democracy, not that every person abandons moral reasoning for team-based moral identity.

Which is predicated on the fact that that's happening. I'm saying you're being too broad, that you can't distinguish between what you're talking about and parties having strong moral positions. If you can't distinguish between what you think is happening and other possibilities, then your conclusion is not useful.

I’m not claiming you can always distinguish between team-based moral identity and genuinely strong moral conviction. I’m saying there are many situations where you clearly can. There are probably a dozen+ examples in this thread alone. If you say it’s impossible to distinguish at all, you’re ignoring observable patterns like asymmetry, goalpost-shifting, or refusal to define standards.
