CMV: Stronger states' rights would benefit everyone politically in the United States Support for stronger states' rights is often seen as a conservative position, and there hasn't really been much support for it in mainstream politics on either side of the aisle. I feel like states should have more power to regulate themselves to reduce polarization, governmental dysfunction, resentment of federal taxes, and generally just to satisfy the political needs of a lot of different people. States really feel like lines on a map at this point, and I think in the process we've lost a key feature of our country that our founders intended. I feel like a strong national government for a country as huge as the United States is just a bad model, but given states' rights aren't really pushed on either side of the aisle, I'd like to hear some perspectives on why that is. I'm sure there's something I'm overlooking.
Basically, the national government is polarized. Congress is often gridlocked, and feels broken to the average citizen because the people in there are just so diametrically opposed. Elections are always close and the other side always resents it when they lose. Commonly proposed solutions to make the government "work" are really just eliminating longstanding checks and balances to make it easier for a 51% majority government to impose drastic changes on a bunch of people who don't consent to them: eliminating the filibuster, packing the Supreme Court, unitary executive theory, legislation from the bench. People overlook the fact that maybe a national government just doesn't work well at this scale and instead just want their party to be able to push the other around and for their voter base to just cry about it until they get the 1% swing state vote next election.
My question is, why can't states take up the mantle and regulate in the way their populace wants? Republicans can live in red states, and Democrats can live in blue states. Red states can make red laws that the people there will all agree with-if you live in Texas, no abortion, lower taxes, subsidies for up-and-coming businesses, freer market, a conservative's utopia. Democrats can make laws that all the people there will agree with-high minimum wage, higher taxes, investigations into companies, pro-choice, anything a Democratic voter could want. Yes, there are divisions within the parties-not all reds or blues want the same things. But it'll still be a much better situation than what we have now, where nobody agrees with anything and people storm the capitol when they lose.
This brings other benefits too. State governments are more responsive to the people, they live closer by, they can set up their own systems (state constitutions), people feel more in touch with their politicians. State congresses can have members from each district, meaning someone who lives 20 minutes from your house usually has real decision making power. Politicians have less people to worry about, and huge scandals aren't constantly tearing up the news because states worry about themselves. People often dislike federal taxation, control, and aid, especially red voters-they want to see their money being spent closer to home. With states setting taxes, that can happen.
Originally, the founders intended America's federal government to be a sort of coalition between states that regulates interstate trade, military protection, currency, and foreign policy. No one state can do any of those things, so it makes sense that a national government, representative of people from those states, can come together to make decisions in those limited areas. It's clear that the federal government was intended to be far less powerful than it is now; however, an abusive interpretation of the commerce clause, plus over-delegation of power from Congress to the President (basically, procedural political hacks that the founding fathers didn't intend at all) has basically allowed the federal government to grow so powerful states can't do anything themselves. I'm basically advocating for a return of enumerated powers-why can't the federal government stick to regulating matters for the whole country, like the military? It should probably have immigration power too, since border states shouldn't be able to control immigration and prevent upper states from getting immigrants purely based on geography.
States can better represent their constituents. The national government should still exist, but it should require bipartisan consensus to get things done, and it should only have the powers granted by the Constitution. Then, people will stop complaining about the President, because the federal government can maybe only do things with ⅔ consensus and only if it affects interstate trade, military, currency, etc. Something like this works in the EU pretty much. It could create political bubbles, but that could be better than arguing and even violence when different parties mix. Travel between states could mean new laws you have to deal with, but just regular travel activity where you don't live there isn't likely to run afoul of laws anyway, right?
I'm open to seeing new perspectives on how this couldn't work politically, practically, or socially. Change my view!
In basically every situation, individual rights are better than states' rights. And expanding the latter usually involves curtailing the former.

Sure, when it implicates Constitutional rights states shouldn't get a say. But what would you say about the Affordable Care Act? No (Constitutional) individual rights are implicated there, and yet it's still a federal law that states have no say in. Or what about economic regulations, where individual rights aren't at stake?

I mean, this is just false. States were responsible for implementing the ACA and had wide latitude in how to do so. That's why outcomes differed so much from state to state. 
But obviously it would be better to just have everyone have an individual right to healthcare, as is the case in countries with lower healthcare costs and better healthcare outcomes than us.
Macroeconomic management can only be done at the federal level because only the federal government can engage in monetary policy.

only the federal government can engage in monetary policy
Yeah, I think that's a bad rule, and states should be able to engage in monetary policy. Officially, the Constitution only lets the federal government coin money, not give them power over everything economic. I'm open to understanding why the federal government needs to have this power. Why couldn't states do it?
obviously it would be better to just have everyone have an individual right to healthcare
You should be able to move to a state where that's a reality. And people who oppose that idea (many do) should be able to move away from that state. Isn't that easier than lobbying the gridlocked, polarized federal government?

Why couldn't states do it?
They can do it, but having each state have its own separate currency creates all sorts of other problems. See Europe before the Euro.
You should be able to move to a state where that's a reality.
No, because if people can just move and get healthcare then all sick people will do that and will bankrupt whatever state tries to set this up.

CMV: Psychiatric misdiagnosis rates are high enough to invalidate the practice of diagnosing all together. It’s is often a requirement for psychiatric care to be covered by medical insurance companies, creating a conflict of interest keeping the broken system alive. Being misdiagnosed can have severe consequences. You’d receive the wrong treatment. The wrong drugs. This is a serious issue that warrants suspension of this practice altogether (in my view);
fixed link to researchgate article
“Diagnostic errors are common and consequential in mental health care. For example, up to 76.8% of people with bipolar disorder and 50% with depressive disorders have been misdiagnosed, leading to delayed or inappropriate treatment and mistrust in services. Complex presentations drive confusion. Symptom overlap, high comorbidity, and the absence of objective biomarkers make differential diagnosis particularly difficult (e.g., bipolar vs. unipolar depression; schizophrenia spectrum vs. other disorders). Clinician and system pressures contribute. Time constraints, cognitive biases, variable training, and systemic incentives (e.g., diagnosis for service access) increase the risk of misclassification in everyday practice.”
76.8% or 50% are disqualifying ratios to me. Like playing Russian roulette with your mental health.
Websites like psychologytoday and openpathcollective list psychiatrists in their directory that have expired licenses and registration, practicing illegally. Openpath even gives them a verified badge, while only verifying them once upon registration and a one time lifetime fee of like 80$ to get listed.
I think the system may be kind of broken at the moment.
I think there are brilliant psychotherapists and other therapists out there, especially transpersonal and hypnotherapy.
Furthermore, what is known in the DSM-V as “moral, religious or spiritual problem”—not considered a mental disorder—also known as “spiritual emergency” as coined by Stanislav Grof, one of the developer of transpersonal psychology. He states in his research paper co-authored by his wife Christina Grof;
“There exists increasing evidence that many individuals experiencing episodes of nonordinary states of consciousness accompanied by various emotional, perceptual, and psychosomatic manifestations are undergoing an evolutionary crisis rather than suffering from a mental disease (Grof, 1985). The recognition of this fact has important practical and theoretical consequences. If properly understood and treated as difficult stages in a natural developmental process, these experiences—spiritual emergencies or transpersonal crises—can result in emotional and psychosomatic healing, creative problem-solving, personality transformation, and consciousness evolution. This fact is reflected in the term “spiritual emergency,” which suggests a crisis, but also suggests the potential for rising to a higher state of being.”
If clinicians fail to recognize a legit spiritual emergency vs psychosis, well frankly the patient is screwed. Drugs that numb the experience and misunderstanding and label, harm to reputation that come with false diagnosis can follow someone for life.
“Psychosis is a central concept in mental health, yet the concept is unclear. Clinicians are challenged with the task to be able to distinguish psychotic phenomena; however, little is known about how clinicians are able to distinguish religious/spiritual phenomena from psychotic phenomena, as both may be similar in presentation”
Fixed link to researchgate article
A 2020 study found therapists often struggle (e.g., 40–60% report needing more training); misdiagnosis leads to stigma or inappropriate meds.
So yeah the whole psychiatric system needs an overhaul, a the medical/insurance establishments as well for that matter. CMV, I’ll delta anyone who changes it even a little. My view is now roughly 80% negative against the current system.
So what is your advice? I have a diagnosis, but my doctor treats the symptoms as I explain them. I think that is a pretty good system. How else would you do it?
A more holistic method. I don’t think formal diagnosis is essential, and drugs are overprescribed.
You might be right about drugs being over prescribed. But what is this holistic method that solves this issue?

More holistic training at colleges and universities. Things like transpersonal psychology are largely ignored. Things like guided imagery, group meditation and trance healing, hypnotherapy, none of its is covered by insurance. More research needs to be done into the healing power of intention and belief as evidenced by placebo trials and well documented cases of spontaneous remission. Also the gut/heart/brain axis and microbiome health is super slept on as well.
Because those things are not what is needed by someone dealing with a serious mental illness.

What is needed? Drugs? How often has a drug cured serious and violent mental illness or ptsd? How often has talk therapy for that matter? At least 88% of our minds are subconscious beliefs and processes. Conscious desires to change will always be defeated by ingrained subconscious beliefs. Have you read about the results of psychedelic PTSD trials?

Drugs don’t cure anything, they treat it. And if you stick with them, they treat it well. Also I don’t know why you are associating serious mental illness with violence. That is not the case the vast majority of the time.

!delta Well the most serious mental illnesses are the violent kind. Even if rare, which I agree it is.
Then don’t make formal diagnosis a requirement to get the drugs you need. Just a your caregivers recommendations.
Exactly drugs don’t cure anything, often they don’t work or have serious side effects, sometimes they make the systems worse. Getting the wrong drugs is even worse.
The truth is people have been cured from a variety of ailments. It’s possible. As psychedelic trials show, or ketamine therapy, etc. the focus needs to change.
But you raised valid arguments on the difficulty and perhaps even naivety of thinking such a utopian scenario is possible.
Negative view against current system lowered to 69%

CMV: Violence does more harm than good. It should be avoided except as a last resort to defend oneself. Violence is a contagion. Due to the way people learn social behaviors -- that is, experiencing the behavior and then imitating it -- violence leads to more violence. When someone is exposed to violence it is much more likely that they then in the future act violently. It's not hard to think of places where normalized violence boiled over into wars, pogroms, genocides, and so many other social ill. We see this all over the place. Violence begets more violence.
The social consequences of violence, whatever you're using it for, are uniformly negatives. With research pointing to increased incidences of depression, anxiety, posttraumatic stress disorder, and suicide; increased risk of cardiovascular disease; and premature mortality. When interpersonal violence spills over into the community the outcomes can reach societal levels. It scares people out of participating in neighborhood activities, limits business growth, strains education, justice, and medical systems, and slows community progress. Violence does more harm than good in almost all cases.
If we come to conclusion that violence spreads like a contagion and that contagion is negative how can we justify acting violently except in the direst scenarios. We, in doing so, necessarily create more violence and thus more social harm.

You’re not going to find many people trying to convince you that violence isn’t negative unless the situation calls for it.
The better question, or crux of the issue, would be when we considered violence acceptable to begin with. What the context and thresholds between acceptable and unacceptable are.
There’ll always be people who’ll use violence as a means to get what they want. And you’ll always need the threat of superior levels of violence to keep those people in check. Ie law enforcement, a better military than the other nation, potential of a bigger person intervening if you decide to mess with people physically.
When boiled down to its most fundamental level, existence as a person or a society comes down to your ability to exert force and violence to defend yourself or your society, tribe or way of life. A world in which we only ever settle things via words will never ever be possible.

I guess that's my point, I don't see violence as legitimate except in defense of yourself or others. That's my threshold. I think you get to the other point I was trying to get at quite well. Can a peaceful world really exist snd I think yes. People can be taught that violence is an evil we should abstain from and very few people actually are. They see violence and even if they heard the words that violence is wrong they may imitate it and find they like it.
But in a society that truly abhors violence the social pressures could be such that violent individuals are mostly cowed into going along with the social order. If they aren't they can always be separated from the group and in that instance I think I would consider it an act of self defense, even though the threat of violence and maybe the enactment of violence is there.

Right but where you see it being legitimate, that may not reach the threshold of someone else’s legitimate, or others are even below yours.
I don’t think peace equates to no threat of violence, arguably a greater underlying threat of violence can create peace. It deters trying in the first place.
Humans are emotional creatures. Sometimes taking things into your own hands when the situation calls for it in your mind, even if not legal, can be justified.

I don’t think peace equates to no threat of violence, arguably a greater underlying threat of violence can create peace. It deters trying in the first place.
But it does equate to no violence, the threat of violence is alwaya there if you transgress againsg another person, yeah.
Violence not yet being inflicted isn’t the same as there being no violence at all

CMV: Most of the problems faced by humans are created by humans themselves Something I have observed, and when I look at the bare necessities, let's take the first and foremost necessity for all life: quality breathable air. Inequality in access to quality breathable or even breathable air with a quality that's tolerable and not fatal to health in the long term. We have this problem because we created it, and other species face it too because of us.
Now I expand the same argument to rest of the necessities human needs to live comfortably in the modern world
We have SDGs for most of this, a goal is a future state/objective to be achieved, After these many years of development, we still haven't solved the problems in bare necessities required to sustain human life.
Water and Food: SDG in progress
Shelter: inadequate and unaffordable housing
Clothing: Limited freedom of clothing for women in some countries
Electricity, transport, communication, education, and employment - Common inequality in access and quality.
I believe this might be due to human overpopulation and/or humanity's disregard for the value of life of its own species (but this is not primary argument now)
We create a problem, then we solve the problem ourselves to appear heroic (again for ourselves) and the vicious cycle repeats over and over again and again. For the life of me I don't understand why we keep this vicious cycle running, it feels like a cruel video game.
So yeah it makes me conclude that most of the problems faced by humans are created by humans themselves
Please change my view, I hope I'm wrong.
No hope for you specifically? What about the rest of humanity?
The rich have more than hope though, I'm just talking about necessities here, the bare necessities.
Why do you think this issue affects you more than the rest of us? I'm not rich.

This issue affects every human being on the planet, I have suffered too and observing this has led me to existential fatigue and negative thoughts which I want to stray away from and so I've been trying to find answers and this post is an attempt

I think if geopolitical issues are causing you to have an existential crisis you are internalizing them too much. If the news is causing you to have negative thoughts and be depressed maybe take a break from it for awhile.
It's not the recent news, I've been feeling this way since 2021. Things only got worse not even a little better.
You have to take care of yourself and your mental health first before you can help sort out world problems.

I know I can't help sort out, but at least I want to understand it better which can make me feel good
I'm part of this vicious cycle whether I like it or not and the least thing I could get is to understand why it happens or when it will correct itself.

CMV: Being drunk should never be an excuse for any unfaithful behavior whatsoever This is something that I believe is too normalized in today's (at least American) society, and something that I believe it also perpetuated by pop culture and media as well. To start off my post I'm gonna refer to The Office to illustrate my point.
In Season 2 Ep 1 of The Office, there is a scene in the episode where, at the Dundies, Pam gets drunk and gives Jim a big kiss. Mind you, Pam is engaged to Roy in this moment. Both the show, the characters, and largely the audience think little of it and push it to the side saying that, since Pam was drunk, it's no matter and not a big deal. I am here to say that, in that moment, Pam cheated on Roy.
I have arrived to this opinion because I truly do not believe that being under the influence should be able to exonerate you from any act that would otherwise unfaithful. Let's look at some other examples-
If you get into a car accident while drunk, will the authorities let you off the hook because you were under the influence? After all, it was just a drunken mistake. What about if you shoplifted while drunk? What about getting into a physical altercation? Do people who do these things get reduced punishments because they are under the influence? Obviously not
If this is how we think of crimes being committed while under the influence, then why does the line of infidelity suddenly become blurry when someone's drunk? Where is the line suddenly moved to when you are drunk? What's ok and what's not now? Is it excusable to kiss someone while drunk? What about make out? What about doing something sexual? Where is the line drawn?
Because of this, being drunk should not ever to any point exonerate someone from cheating on their partner or being unfaithful in any capacity, with my only exception being if they are intoxicated beyond the point where they can reasonably consent.

being drunk should not ever to any point exonerate someone
Does this apply to other intoxicants or just alcohol?
Does it apply to when you didn't know or intend to become intoxicated?

Does this apply to other intoxicants or just alcohol?
Any that would intoxicate you in general
Does it apply to when you didn't know or intend to become intoxicated?
No being roofied is nonconsensual and thus doesn't count

Why not? You said being drunk doesn't change things. Why does the fact that you didn't choose to get drunk do so. Was the "choosing to get drunk when you know you might kiss someone" the part that's cheating?

I mean I don't know about the decision making side of it I guess, but legally roofying someone is very illegal, and people largely do it to people to get them to do sexual things they otherwise wouldn't have done

Sure. Since in your OP you said "with my only exception being if they are intoxicated beyond the point where they can reasonably consent," being roofied isn't really what I meant. I mean -- you didn't know that what you were drinking was as alcoholic as it was, or didn't know it was alcoholic at all. You're not so slammed that you couldn't understand what's going on, but you are, unintentionally, a bit drunk now. Does your initial opinion still apply?

CMV: Lacking confidence is not a life sentence. Lots of people have varying baselines of innate confidence. My kid is probably the most confident, charismatic person I’ve ever met. That being said, I absolutely could see how someone in her class could become more confident than her if they practice, and if she keeps relying on her natural confidence to carry her throughout life.
It’s like she has a natural +8 confidence stat. Right now she’s way ahead of a lot of others, but as people see her and want to compete with her, and as people get into their own things and gain confidence through learning skills, then she’ll potentially stay right around that +8 while lots of other kids keep getting consistent increases, until lots of them are well over +8.
It’s like those people that can lift a lot without going to the gym very much. Genetically dense muscles and bones, that kind of thing. They get a head start but they can be outlifted. It isn’t easy, but it can be done.
Confidence isn’t just something you have or you don’t have. It’s something you practice. If you practice it then that doesn’t mean you don’t have it. Even people that are very confident still read their presentation notes over and over again to make sure they make as few mistakes as humanly possible. The confidence they show when they’re presenting didn’t just happen overnight. They practiced overnight, often obsessively.

This is one of those classic “nature vs nurture” arguments.
You’re 100% correct, some people are more confident than others and that confidence can fluctuate based on their experiences and current phase of life.
However, there are also people with natural personality traits that predispose them to projecting confidence, or to not be outwardly confident. To use your example, I’m an extrovert, and I’ve had to present before my colleagues and at medical conferences many times. I can count on one hand how many times I’ve reread a presentation that I mentally called “done”. I’ve never done it obsessively. In my brain, when a task is finished I don’t need to “practice” it anymore.
Great post btw, thank God we can discuss something that isn’t politics

Is that part of your process? You read and practice it once or twice and then actively try not to obsess over it, and all that makes it more natural sounding? Or is it something a little less calculated?

I honestly don’t think about it that much. I spend a lot of time during the “doing” phase, and very little past that. It’s how my brain works. When I was a kid in school, I wrote papers once, proofread, and submitted them. I never understood the need for “rough drafts”, in the same way that I don’t see the need to practice a presentation. In my brain, I know how to speak, and if I’ve put sufficient effort into a presentation, it doesn’t need to be rehearsed

Ok so then yeah you’ve got that base level confidence that’s already pretty high and it seems like it’s working out for you so far. Thats awesome!
!delta because I guess you’re one of those people that’s really confident and doesn’t spend extra time trying to get something right.
All I’ll say is that if you don’t keep it up on purpose, someone can surpass you. And I mean there are billions of people so that doesn’t really matter in the long run, but you may be up against someone else for a promotion one day, and someone will get it because they practiced more and you didn’t feel like you needed to.

All very true! I’m a huge fan of putting effort into things, and am something of a workaholic. I think where my mentality defers from yours is that I think the work should be frontloaded rather than revised. The counter argument to your point would be that, with my way, I’m always ready to go. The person who needs time to practice and prepare might miss opportunities because they ran out of time

CMV: Lacking confidence is not a life sentence. Lots of people have varying baselines of innate confidence. My kid is probably the most confident, charismatic person I’ve ever met. That being said, I absolutely could see how someone in her class could become more confident than her if they practice, and if she keeps relying on her natural confidence to carry her throughout life.
It’s like she has a natural +8 confidence stat. Right now she’s way ahead of a lot of others, but as people see her and want to compete with her, and as people get into their own things and gain confidence through learning skills, then she’ll potentially stay right around that +8 while lots of other kids keep getting consistent increases, until lots of them are well over +8.
It’s like those people that can lift a lot without going to the gym very much. Genetically dense muscles and bones, that kind of thing. They get a head start but they can be outlifted. It isn’t easy, but it can be done.
Confidence isn’t just something you have or you don’t have. It’s something you practice. If you practice it then that doesn’t mean you don’t have it. Even people that are very confident still read their presentation notes over and over again to make sure they make as few mistakes as humanly possible. The confidence they show when they’re presenting didn’t just happen overnight. They practiced overnight, often obsessively.

I think you're right in some ways and wrong in others. Can anyone develop confidence? Yes. But as someone who was bullied viciously through my adolescence both at home and in the world, it's not so easy as simply flipping a switch and practicing. Once you've been abused, betrayed, hurt and told that that stems from your own failures, your brain is primed to look for failures and deficiencies. It'll turn basic interactions into a horrorshow of your own failure, and it's extremely haed to see outside of that reaction.
So I think this overall misses the challenges people have, especially children, who don't have the perspective to know it's not them but other people that are the problem. If it was so easy as practice and not literally rewiring the brain, this view would stand true, but at the moment it seems like well-wishing and toxic positivity.

I never said it was easy. If you have trauma to overcome then of course that makes it even harder.
It’s work.

What I'm saying is that it's not just a matter of practice, and it absolutely can be a life long struggle due to these things. It's a matter of deep work with techniques most people don't know or have access to.

The practice is part of the work. And yeah it can be a lot harder for people, but I think a lot of times people just think that something will be harder for them oftentimes either give up before they start or they practice in such a way that’s not really good for the way their minds work. And you’re right maybe they just lack the tools, but if someone is doing the same thing over and over and still failing then at some point it’s their fault that they’re not trying to do some aspect of it a little bit differently. You don’t have to already have the tools at your disposal to know to stop using a pair of pliers to screw in a screw.

What about the people that do try but don't find success? Do they just not exist? I brought up abuse precisely because of this. Some people never get out from under their abuse and that's legitimate and not their fault.

Of course they exist. First of all, what kind of success are we talking about? Personal success in accomplishing a task they set out to do, or success like they became millionaires? I ask because not accomplishing something you set out to do and not becoming a millionaire are both things that happen to people even without trauma.
If they exist then a lack of confidence categorically can be a life-long sentence then, no?

It CAN be, sure, in the same way that having a thorn in your arm can be a lifelong sentence if you never try to remove it. If you want a delta for being ultra specific and identifying that it can sometimes be a life sentence then I’ll give it to you but I’m hoping we can continue this conversation and not have it just be about deltas, because accepting that it can be a life sentence is pretty different from what I’m trying to say with the post. What I’m trying to say is essentially that it never HAS to be a life sentence.

I'm never really in it for the delta, no, so don't give it to me if you don't feel I've changed your view. It won't bother me at all, friend. The post's title is misleading then I will say, but that's neither here nor there.
I just think your view lacks empathy. It places too much blame on the person lacking confidence, when blame will only exasperate the problem they already have. I think a better view is that most people are confident until life gives them cause not to be. In that light it makes more sense to focus on the causes of low confidence, which I think are mostly external -- uncertain parents and parental relationships, scarcity, abuse, legitimate failure etc.
And I say this as a person who's confidence has fluctuated widely through their lives and have had to build what I confidence I have through the means you elucidate. I ain't saying you're exactly wrong, just missing what people are going through.

It isn’t about blame, but if someone lacks confidence due to being bullied then it’s the bullies’ fault, not the fault of the person bullied. That being said, if you want to accomplish something that you know will require confidence to do, let’s say public speaking, then there are steps that you can take to get better at it, and the process of getting better at it will make you more confident. The steps to getting better at it are no less available than guitar lessons are, meaning that they are widely available.

The same could be said for abortion, homosexuality, and pre-marital sex. It has undoubtedly persisted past the cotton gin, feudalism, and the wheel, showing it's persistent role in society. If humans found it abhorrent, the profession have dissipated long before we started forming empires.
Presenting potentially counter argument: "Yes, but so have wars, human atrocities, mercenaries, and child predators."
First off, if you equate prostitution with any of those darker sides of humanity--check yourself. Two adults agreeing on a mutual agreement through sex soars above people like Epstein. They've only been placed together because religious society has equated them as such. Liberalism has also advocated against it because of the horror stories from personal account, and while albeit true that it has lead to some dark turns--if openly practiced and supervised, it'd be unlikely that incidents aforementioned would be much closer to zero.
If intercourse was regulated, taxed, and unionized--it'd be no different than wanting to kill yourself for working at a gas station (disregarding the carnal element). Granted, exploitation occurs--but say that to any other field and your left in the dust. I'm a 32 year old man, not an edgy teenager for reference, and would I personally use it if set in the given parameters? Absolutely.
Edit; for those who downvote without argumentative retort, I'll take it as a concession of you cannot defend your stance.

Studies have shown that legal prostitution increases exploitation and sex trafficking. Because it's all part of the sex trade. Also many of the "consenting" adult prostitutes are driven into the sex trade by abusive boyfriends, addiction, mental health issues or other factors.
I also don't understand your claim that the only objections come from a "religious or ethical angle." Leaving aside religion for a moment, aren't all laws based on ethics and morality? Are you really arguing that ethics and morality are not a good reason to make something illegal? In that case why is murder illegal? Or robbery?

Maybe narrowing my points will help. Much of Western civilization is based on the Christian gospel, a derivative of Judaic law and tradition. Across the board, everyone can agree that common murder and theft are bad. However, what it significantly more murky is prostitution. The Roman Empire, Western societies cultural patriarch, was even very see-saw about this issue, but much like Christianity, their arguments game from a religious perspective. It just so happened to be codified by the arrival of Christianity AND EVEN THEN, the punishments were significantly less harsh than murder or theft (give or take a few zealous rulers). CMV: A Lunar Colony is a good idea We finally seem to be entering the era of space colonization, with major well funded proposals for lunar bases, colonies, and exploration of Mars and Venus in preparation to establish permanent bases and colonies. right now it seems rather likely that the us and china will each have a moon base or colony withen the next 20 years. i think these are clearly good ideas an investments for the future of humanity.
the first reason I think these are a good idea is environmental. to maintain industrial society we need ever increasing amounts of processed minerals, producing them is highly polluting and requires dragging them out of the ground. theres always going to be enviromental damage there, and luna has pretty much an identical composition to earth. meaning we can skip all the ecological damage and pollution by shifting producting of these things to the moon
luna is also the natural staging area for deep space exploration, its significantly easier to launch things from the moon then from earth, so as long as we are planning on settling the rest of the solar system or atleast exploring it, a lunar base makes sense.
space exploration has also produced huge breakthroughs in science. things like scratch resistant lens, weather forcasting, and renewable energy all came from space exploration. setting up bases on foreign worlds would by necessity require solving problems similar to those faced in living at high latitudes. opening up areas like siberia, greenland, alaska, northern canada, and Antarctica to more permanent human settlement, and improving the lives of those who live there. and thats just the foreseeable stuff.

The moon has a significant impact on Earth.
Probably the best reason not to do a lunar colony, at least of the kind you're imagining is that anything we can do to the moon has a high probability of affecting the Earth if it goes wrong on a big enough scale.
The moon controls the tides, is really important for animals and plants, is half of all the light we get to see, and I'm not sure what else we could lose if we destroyed the moon.
It wouldn't be a bad place to test the concept of colonies, but really the best we can do is use different planets.
Also, we thus far have not found a way to travel through space in a way that would make any sense to mine for resources. Those resources would be worth many times less than they cost to gather. The only benefit would be to use it in space to scale down the losses of trying to build more. But then you would probably build it on one of the moons of the planet you're using.
The biggest reason is survival, but the main reason that billionaires like Musk and Bezos want it is that it allocated all of the resources in their pockets and then allows them to control the outcome.
I would say between them, Musk seems to want to believe it, but the problem with that is that he is basically pretty open that he wants to be supreme overlord. Everyone that comes to Mars is a slave to the Martian colony and he controls them.
Bezos, I believe, doesn't really care. It's just that he has to have a justification to explain that he owns rockets so he made something up about factories in space.
Our best model of survival is to work out how to move humanity towards intelligent use of resources and manage a climate in the habitable world we have. This avoids all the problems of risking death on other planets every time the big tent gets torn.
These are not impossible problems. It just requires the conscious effort to start to make the changes.

Im not sure how a moon colony could disrupt the tides... it could definitely affect moon phases, but disrupting the tides is something thats totally outside the realm of plausiblity.
Historically while corporations often lead the way on colonizing missions, they usually dont stay in charge and as more and more wealth comes in its harder and harder to maintain a closed off system. Several modern us states were origionally corporate colonies where as they grew the wealth generated fell outside the hands of the corporation and eventually transitioned to democracies.
Your point on deep space travel is valid ill give a !delta for that. While there are proposals and theoretical systems for doing it, we dont have them yet. (The most promising is probably nasas sub light warp drive to allow travel at c fractional speeds but that is still probably decades off)

The problem is that we view the earth as a place that is sacred. There is a limit to what we can do on Earth. Because we live on Earth.
Even so, we treat the Earth incredibly badly and are relatively uninterested in what happens to it.
The moon has no such precautions.
It feels weird to say we have to imagine total devastation but we already did that here in the form of nuclear bombs and disasters, climate change, the ozone layer.
We are going to do a lot of things we shouldn't do and we don't have the imagination to imagine how badly that could go.
If you go to another planet, the scale of things is magnified by another level.
I'm not sure total destruction of Mars would be good thing for the solar system, but it's a hell of a lot harder to do and a hell of a lot further away.

Total destruction? I think you are a overestimating humanities power. We dont have the ability to death star a planet, and earth will keep spinning no matter what we do. One day we'll be gone and a new group of species will have risen to take our place.

I think we have to appreciate the reality that we don't actually know the limits of humanity's power.
Most of nuclear history has been spent finding out rapidly what can go wrong and the scale of that.
If you engage seriously with the thought experiment, we are talking about next level tech that can do things on a planetary scale.
Just because it would take something huge doesn't mean we couldn't do it. Actually, that's kind of the concern.
The guardrails on Earth are that we might actually do something dangerous and make Earth uninhabitable, even small parts of it. We have still done and are still doing so much more.
The moon has no guardrails and lots of apparent breathing room for whatever stupid thing you want to do. A lot of processes are exponential and not necessarily possible to bring under control.
I think my main argument is that given the moon provides useful functions to the Earth, which I imagine we still need at this stage, it would be dangerous to start testing out planetary scale plans on the basis that you could screw up the moon in hitherto unforeseen ways and screw up the Earth.
I think the people who are going "prove it" are missing the point. Namely, human nature is to take risks. That's how we got to the moon and it's also how massive disasters we are still recovering from happened.
It's the great and terrible nature of humanity.

CMV: light trucks (SUVs and pickups) should require a separate type of driver's license. I'm using my neck of the woods as a for instance, but I'm sure your local system is similar.
Class G license - cars, vans, etc
Class A - tractor trailers
Class B - school busses
Class M - motorcycles
(And so forth)
I would argue that class G should be insufficient for driving light duty trucks.
The Ford F-series is the number one selling "car" in both US and Canada. It's clearly being marketed as a general "all purpose" car but is it really?
Here are just a few reasons why I think you should need a different class of license to drive pickup trucks and SUVs.
1.Light duty trucks are dangerous
Not just bikes reports
If you’re walking and you get hit by an SUV, you’re 3 times more likely to die than if you’d been hit by a regular car. And if you’re in that regular car, you’re more likely to die in a crash if it’s with an SUV. SUVs are more likely to hit people in the first place, because they’re big, unwieldy, and have poor visibility. SUV drivers themselves are twice as likely to be killed in a rollover than car drivers.
They aren't "cars" because they don't confirm to the same safety standards or CAFE standards as normal cars.
They also aren't crash compatible with normal cars.
They kill kids Again from Not just bikes
It might be easier to see farther down the road, but it’s actually harder to see stuff right in front of you. The advocacy group Kids and Cars put 17 children in front of an SUV, and they couldn’t see any of them from the driver's seat. Since the introduction of SUVs there has been a massive increase in what are called “frontovers”: a person, usually a child, getting run over by an SUV by a driver who can’t even see them. Kids and Cars have been documenting the rise of frontovers in America, and the results are shocking, But before we move on, I want you to look at this chart and understand what you’re seeing. This is saying that over a 10-year  period, over 500 American children were killed by being run over by SUVs, usually  by their own parents in their own driveways.
4x4 mode makes it easier to get going but not to stop. A driver used to 2 wheel drive, may not fully understand how to drive a 4x4, especially in slippery conditions.
Vans and station wagons hold more, so there no argument that your average class G driver needs a light truck.
I could go on, but I'm really curious what arguments you could make that a basic class G license should be sufficient for driving these massive vehicles.
So CMV. Why should the same license that let's me drive my Fiat also let me drive a Palisade?
And how would a separate piece of plastic keep anyone safer?

Couple of ways
1) there would be less of them if you requires a special class of license
2) the people who would be aware of how unsafe they are.
Right now they are marked as being safer, but a separate class of license could clarify that they are much less safe.
We need to flip that mind set.

Why would this special license be harder to get? You know people rarely get pulled over when driving so there's not really any incentive to get a special license plenty of people might just do it illegally if you make it difficult to acquire.

Ideally yes.
I think at a minimum, they should have to complete the test in the biggest vehicle in the class, and navigate an obstacle course without cameras or sensors.
Maybe drive on a slick service and be required to stop at a certain distance.

CMV: The narcoterrorism happening in Mexico right now is only making Nayib Bukele’s strategy more attractive As you may or may not know, Mexico damn near descended into chaos the other day after a military operation that resulted in the killing of one cartel boss, El Mencho, took place. In retaliation, the cartel, CJNG, went on a terrorism spree, shoot*ng up the international airport of Guadalajara, att*cking grocery stores, k*lling innocent civilians, wreaking havoc for havocs’ sake. They weren’t even targeting military personnel, they were intentionally terrorizing civilians to make going against the cartel unpopular, literally forcing the civilian populace into submission by fear. Hundreds of thousands, possibly millions, of Mexicans throughout the country were forced to stay in their houses or risk being purposely shot by the cartel.
I was listening to someone talk about their experience and how they’re managing, and they ended their video with a surprising “fuck the president”. This shocked me, because I know that Claudia Sheinbaum enjoys a relatively high approval rating in Mexico for the most part. But apparently, and to the dismay of countless Mexicans, she’s more lax on the cartels than people would prefer. She calls the war against the narcos “unlawful” because it’s killing without trial and only increases the homicide rate in Mexico (which is true), and says that the war against narcos is something that “the conservatives” want but that her administration won’t partake in.
And I get it. In an ideal world, everyone suspected of committing a crime — even terrorists— would face due process and go through a fair trial. But after what happened (and what is still going on), there gets to a point where the masses might stop caring about “the right to a fair trial” if it gets them results like El Salvador quicker. People are literally looking at El Salvador and saying that theirs is a model Mexico needs to follow. It’s sad. There are people saying that a couple of false arrests and convictions is a price they’re willing to pay to eliminate the narcos for good.
I’m not going to pretend I know the solution to the narco problem. But it really does look like the patience of the masses is running out (or has already run out), and sooner or later, pro-Bukele sentiment is going to popularize in Mexico. An alternative to defeating the narcos quickly needs to happen immediately.

CMV: International law is mostly meaningless because powerful states can ignore it. I understand the desire for international law. It would be nice if the world could just sing kumbaya every time the UN met and everyone came to an agreement on the major issues facing humanity. But unfortunately, that’s not how things work.
In reality, the system is dominated by the states that have the most power (US, Russia, China) and state who have veto power in the UN Security Council (US, Russia, China). Because of that, they can’t really be bound by international law in any meaningful sense. Who is actually going to stop them?
On top of that, these states fundamentally disagree on major political and ideological issues, which means there will never be real consensus on what international “law” actually is.
In my opinion, the only way true international law could exist would be if there were a genuine world government with an executive authority capable of enforcing laws on the ground across the globe. But I don’t think anyone wants this.
So my conclusion is that the UN is basically being held together by the threat of nuclear warfare and the possibility of human extinction. As long as nuclear weapons exist, the major powers have an incentive to maintain some kind of diplomatic framework. But if that underlying threat somehow disappeared, I think the UN would crumble almost instantly.
How do we solve the problem of nukes? I have no idea, (and apparently no one else does). So the UN will probably continue to exist. But in practice, it feels more like a puppet show than a system that actually governs anything—and anyone who thinks it is truly law in the world is, in my view, mistaken.

you do make a good point that there are important international laws, but the UN... that's a bunch of nonsnese. they did nothing for rwanda in 1994, what are they doing in any of these current conflicts now? its dumb.

There are many UN committees that facilitate the orderly creation, amendment and maintenance of treaties. The UN is just one organization to assist nations in organizing and collaborating in creating treaties.
The Hague Conference on Private International Law (which I've now cited twice) regularly collaborates via the UN. It's an official observer at the UN General Assembly and is extremely influential.
For some reason, people see the UN as this distinct thing separate from its member nations. The UN is just a platform for nations to communicate and organize with and against each other. There isn't really a UN with power distinct from its member states.

cmv: Good people don’t become cops anymore. (In the U.S.) This post refers mostly to regular, uniformed, “patrolman” style cops in the U.S.
I thought about this while watching the viral video of the cops (and congressman) break that marines arm for speaking out during a hearing. The force they initiated was so extreme right off the bat that it seemed almost personal. In fact, I’ve seen dozens of videos of cops arresting journalists, mothers, grandparents, kids, etc at town halls (which is disturbing) and I have never seen one this egregious.
It makes me wonder, who signs up for a job like that? Knowing all the hate law enforcement receives, all the problems plaguing police culture, the unpopular war on drugs, the fact you might be ordered to arrest a marine for protesting law makers, why would you still sign up? The job literally encourages lying and trickery. Cops are constantly looking for new ways to obtain your ID or search your car without consent.
I have a theory. They sign up because they think it’s cool. Maybe their dad, uncle, neighbor or someone they looked up to was a cop and they always wanted to be one. They want that uniform, they want the car, the gun, the title, and the power. The good guy routine, the “I want to serve my community” line, is just what you have to say to get the job. Anyone who signed up because they actually care about justice or the constitution would have quit during the academy or early on in training.
Good people don’t do bad things behind the guise of “I’m just doing my job.” Policing draws in narcissists. (Source: https://www.lexipol.com/resources/blog/controlling-your-ego-in-police-work/) The vast majority of officers are conservative republicans. A political ideology often associated with a lack of empathy. One disqualifying factor for becoming an officer in most states is an IQ limitation. I don’t believe these things are completely coincidental but are by design.

I think focusing on whether individual cops are good or bad misses the systemic incentives. 
For example, if officers are informally pressured to meet quotas, that encourages them to stop or ticket people even when it’s low value, because that’s how they meet expectations. Even a normal person who just wants to keep their job will respond to those incentives. 
And if there’s little accountability for bad behavior, that also shapes behavior because the risk of consequences is low.
Institutions tend to reward people who fit their incentives and filter out people who don’t. Over time that means the system reproduces the same behaviors regardless of who the individuals are. 
So the bigger issue is the incentive structure of the institution, not just the morality of the individuals in it.
You said it better than I could have

I think where I disagree is that I don't think you need to assume policing mostly attracts narcissists or bad people to get these outcomes.
If the incentives reward arrests, discourage whistleblowing, and rarely punish misconduct, then even fairly normal people will drift towards those people over time just to keep their job or adcance.
The reason why I think that matters is because if the problem is mainly "bad people join", the solution is just hiring better people. But if the structure pushes behavior in that direction, you'll keep getting the same results no matter who you hire.

CMV: Inheritance tax is not reducing inequality — in fact, it is increasing it. A common debate in many countries is whether increasing inheritance taxes would reduce inequality in the world. In fact, both the United States and many European countries have implemented high inheritance taxes over the past decades. However, what we have actually seen is an even greater concentration of wealth, despite these measures.
What is really happening is the following: the very wealthy have estate planning. They can protect their assets through corporate structures and legal entities, significantly reducing the tax burden their children will pay. In addition, they have liquidity—that is, cash available in banks to deal with these taxes when the time comes.
Now consider what happens in most upper-middle-class, middle-class, and lower-income families. Many parents spent their lives building or buying a home, and today these homes may be worth a great deal of money. However, that does not mean these families are wealthy. It simply means they own a fixed asset that has been heavily inflated due to poor economic policies and globalization. Most of them do not have enough money in the bank to pay the inheritance tax—neither the parents nor the children.
So what happens in the end? The children are forced to sell the house quickly, often below market value, in order to pay the inheritance tax and the capital gains tax. And who usually buys the property? Very often it ends up being purchased by a real estate investment fund or by a wealthy individual who will rent the property to another family.
In other words, this is a phenomenon happening in both rich and emerging countries, and by itself it contributes to increasing inequality and the concentration of wealth among fewer families.
Throughout my life, I have seen many families go through the painful situation of having to sell their family home in order to pay this tax. In my view, it is destroying one of the few assets the middle class still has. Ironically, this may also explain why many wealthy individuals advocate for higher inheritance taxes — because in practice it is the middle class that will be most affected, allowing wealthy buyers and investment funds to acquire property more cheaply at the very moment when a grieving family is most vulnerable.

But this is exactly where the problem in the discourse appears: even when the tax is progressive, it already starts very high for smaller assets.
Two million in cash sitting in a bank account is a lot of money. But for a house that was built in the 1990s and later appreciated because of inflation and globalization, that value does not necessarily mean the family became richer because of it. In many cases, the children will likely not have the financial means to pay the tax, and the parents themselves will not have enough cash available either, since retirement income is rarely sufficient to build substantial savings.
People with greater wealth and multiple assets can engage in estate planning, which allows them to protect their wealth. In addition, they usually have significant liquidity — enough cash on hand to cover potential inheritance taxes when they arise.
